### FILE: buy-side-cim-analysis-memo.docx
          CONFIDENTIAL — INTERNAL MEMORANDUM  
       Buy-Side CIM Analysis: Cascade Environmental Solutions, Inc. (    “    Project Cascade    ”    )  
                          To:  
          Marcus Yuen, Partner (Deal Lead); Diane Holbrook, Managing Partner  
                From:  
          David Koh, Associate  
                CC:  
          Sarah Lindgren, Vice President  
                Date:  
          June 25, 2025  
                Re:  
          Buy-side analysis of the Confidential Information Memorandum and supporting materials for Cascade Environmental Solutions, Inc. (    “    CES    ”         or the         “    Company    ”    )  
                Process:  
          Sell-side process run by Greenleaf Advisory Group, LLC; IOI deadline July 14, 2025  
                Sources reviewed:  
          (i) CIM dated June 9, 2025; (ii) Process Letter dated June 9, 2025; (iii) Customer Summary & Backlog Report; (iv) Financial Exhibits (Excel); (v) Thornfield Sector Memo dated June 12, 2025  
         
        1. Executive Summary  
       1.1 Deal Snapshot  
                         Item  
          Detail  
            Target  
          Cascade Environmental Solutions, Inc. (Delaware C-corp; HQ Portland, OR)  
            Founder / CEO / Owner  
          Randall Oakes (age 61; 68% equity; transitioning 18–24 months post-close)  
            FY2024 Revenue  
          $87.3M (12.5% CAGR FY21–FY24)  
            FY2024 Reported EBITDA  
          $12.1M (13.9% margin)  
            FY2024 Adjusted EBITDA (as stated)  
          $17.1M (19.6% margin) —             see §4.3; does not foot  
            Segments  
          Environmental Remediation $48.2M (55.2%); Industrial Cleaning $27.8M (31.8%); Emergency Response $11.3M (12.9%)  
            Employees  
          412  
            Contracted backlog / pipeline  
          $42.6M / $31.4M ($74.0M combined)  
            Total debt (per CIM)  
          $17.3M (term loan $14.2M + revolver $3.1M); cash $2.3M  
            Seller’s implied EV  
          $136.8M–$171.0M (8.0x–10.0x FY2024 Adj. EBITDA); $157.6M–$197.0M forward  
            Structure  
          100% equity sale; stock purchase; debt to be refinanced/repaid at close  
            Timeline  
          IOI due July 14, 2025; mgmt presentations Aug 2025; signing/closing Q4 2025  
           1.2 Key Findings  
      CES is a genuinely attractive lower-middle-market environmental services platform with a defensible integrated service model, real regulatory tailwinds (notably PFAS), and a credible 16-year operating track record.             The business merits advancing to an IOI.         However, the marketing materials contain a number of material financial inconsistencies and aggressive assumptions that, taken together, meaningfully affect both the earnings base used for valuation and the cash-flow profile a financial sponsor would underwrite. The most important are:  
             The headline Adjusted EBITDA of $17.1M does not foot.         Reported EBITDA of $12.1M plus the six listed adjustments totaling $4.7M equals             $16.8M, not $17.1M         — a $0.3M overstatement that appears in both the CIM and the Financial Exhibits. At an 8.0x–10.0x multiple this is $2.4M–$3.0M of enterprise value.  
             The CIM’s summary financials and the detailed Financial Exhibits do not reconcile         across the income statement (COGS, gross margin, operating income, net income), the balance sheet (cash, PP&E, total assets, debt, equity), and the cash flow statement. The Exhibits’ income statement is also internally inconsistent: reported EBIT of $11.1M plus D&A of $4.2M implies EBITDA of $15.3M, yet EBITDA is stated as $12.1M (a $3.2M unreconciled gap).  
             Stated DSO of 52 days is unsupported.         Accounts receivable of $16.9M against $87.3M of revenue implies             ~71 days    , not 52 — above the 45–55 day sector norm and the 60-day threshold that signals collection risk. The 52-day figure would require AR of only ~$12.4M.  
             Maintenance capex of $3.0M (3.4% of revenue) is well below the 5–7% sector range         ($4.4M–$6.1M) and is presented as             declining         as a percentage of revenue — a pattern consistent with pre-sale capex deferral observed in Thornfield’s GreenWorks and Allied Waste investments. Normalizing to ~$5.5M reduces annual free cash flow by ~$2.5M.  
             PFAS growth projections are aggressive.         FY2025E PFAS revenue of $14.0M implies 125.8% growth for a two-year-old practice, versus the 30–50% company-level rate indicated by sector data and Thornfield’s GreenWorks analog. PFAS is modeled to drive ~64% of incremental revenue growth through FY2026E, yet no contracted PFAS backlog breakout is provided.  
             The $1.2M regulatory add-back is likely overstated.         Sector and portfolio data indicate an ongoing regulatory/compliance run-rate of $200K–$500K; only the excess should be added back, reducing the defensible add-back to ~$0.7M–$0.9M.  
             The largest customer’s MSA expires March 31, 2025 — before the IOI deadline.         Pacific Northwest Refining Co. (PNR) is 21.4% of revenue; renewal status is the single most urgent diligence item.  
             Customer-identity and relationship-tenure data conflict between the CIM and the Backlog Report         for the entire bottom half of the top-10 (customers #6–#10 are different companies in the two documents) and for several start dates in the top 5.  
        1.3 Recommendation  
          Proceed to submit a non-binding IOI by July 14, 2025    , with valuation anchored on a buyer-normalized Adjusted EBITDA of             ~$16.0M–$16.5M         (FY2024) rather than the seller’s $17.1M, and a multiple of             8.0x–9.0x         reflecting the concentration, capex, PFAS-execution, and key-person risks identified herein. This implies an enterprise value indication of approximately             $130M–$148M    , with a path to the upper end contingent on satisfactory resolution of the Phase II diligence items in §7. The IOI should be explicitly conditional on (a) confirmation of the PNR contract renewal, (b) a PFAS contracted-backlog breakout, (c) a fleet age/condition and capex assessment, and (d) a 10-year regulatory enforcement history. Detailed guidance is in §8–9.  
      
         2. Company Overview  
      CES is a Pacific Northwest environmental services platform founded in 2009 by Randall Oakes and headquartered at 4200 Industrial Way, Portland, OR. The Company operates across Oregon, Washington, Idaho, and Northern California from three facilities (Portland HQ/yard, Seattle office, Boise depot) and holds hazardous waste transporter permits in all four states plus an Oregon DEQ Contractor License (ENV-2009-04821). CES employs 412 people, including two licensed Professional Engineers (down from three after a January 2025 retirement).  
          Ownership.         Delaware C-corp; Oakes holds 68%; the remaining 32% is held by early investors and key employees; no institutional investors. A phantom equity plan for senior managers is expected to be settled in             cash at closing         (a real cash outflow to be sized in the equity bridge, not merely a non-cash add-back).  
          Segments (FY2024):  
                           Segment  
          Revenue ($M)  
          % of Total  
          Profile  
            Environmental Remediation  
          48.2  
          55.2%  
          Soil/groundwater, UST, Phase I/II, brownfield, PFAS; PE-overseen; multi-year project lifecycles  
            Industrial Cleaning  
          27.8  
          31.8%  
          Tank cleaning, hydroblasting, vacuum trucks, confined space; recurring turnaround cycles; EMR 0.87  
            Emergency Response  
          11.3  
          12.9%  
          24/7 hazmat spill response; premium pricing; inherently variable/event-driven  
                Total  
              87.3  
              100%  
       
             Management.         Oakes (CEO/Founder, 61) intends to transition out of day-to-day operations within 18–24 months post-close. Janet Prewitt (VP Operations, 18-year tenure) oversees the largest segment and holds key client relationships. Other principals: Derek Cahill (VP Sales, since 2014), Lisa Fontaine (Controller, since 2016), Mark Stovall (Safety Director, since 2012). Audited by Ridgeline Accounting Group, PC; seller counsel Wexford, Mallory & Chen LLP.  
      
        3. Investment Thesis / Attractive Attributes  
              Integrated, regulated platform with real barriers to entry.         Four-state hazardous waste transporter permits, DEQ contractor license, PE-stamped remediation work, and a purpose-built environmental staging yard are not quickly replicated. The integrated remediation/cleaning/emergency-response model drives cross-selling and share-of-wallet.  
              Genuine growth track record.         12.5% revenue CAGR (FY21–FY24) is above the 5–10% Pacific Northwest sector norm, even after discounting the PFAS contribution. Reported EBITDA margin expanded from 12.1% to 13.9%.  
              Regulatory tailwinds.         EPA’s 2024 PFAS drinking water rule and Oregon/Washington state initiatives create a multi-decade demand cycle for investigation and remediation. CES is an early mover with dedicated PFAS sampling/treatment equipment.  
              Revenue visibility and stickiness.         $42.6M contracted backlog; ~90% MSA/service-agreement renewal rate; no top-10 customer lost in five years; high switching costs (safety qualifications, regulatory knowledge, on-site integration).  
              Fragmented market with consolidation runway.         Management has identified 5–7 tuck-in targets; CES can serve as a regional platform, consistent with Fund III’s strategy.  
              Fit with Fund III mandate.         Environmental services is a core Fund III end market, and Thornfield has direct operating experience via GreenWorks Remediation and Allied Waste Systems (Fund II) that is directly applicable.  
      
        4. Financial Analysis  
       4.1 Historical Performance (per CIM)  
                           ($M)  
          FY2021  
          FY2022  
          FY2023  
          FY2024  
            Revenue  
          61.4  
          68.9  
          76.1  
          87.3  
            YoY growth  
          —  
          12.2%  
          10.4%  
          14.7%  
            Reported EBITDA  
          7.4  
          8.6  
          10.3  
          12.1  
            EBITDA margin  
          12.1%  
          12.5%  
          13.5%  
          13.9%  
            Adjusted EBITDA (stated)  
          9.7  
          11.1  
          13.4  
          17.1  
            Net income (CIM)  
          2.7  
          3.2  
          4.1  
          5.0  
            Free cash flow (CIM)  
          2.2  
          2.3  
          2.0  
          2.0  
         Reported EBITDA margin expansion (12.1% → 13.9%) is credible and within the 10–15% sector range. The Adjusted EBITDA margin of 19.6%, however, sits at the upper boundary of plausibility for this sector (sector memo benchmark: 15–18% adjusted) and is driven entirely by add-backs that require independent validation (see §4.3).  
        4.2 Segment Revenue — CIM vs. Financial Exhibits  
      FY2024 segment totals reconcile, but the             historical segment split (FY2021–FY2023) differs         between the CIM and the Exhibits, and the             projection segment mix differs         (the CIM leans more heavily on the volatile Emergency Response line). Selected differences:  
                           ($M)  
          CIM FY2021  
          Exhibits FY2021  
          CIM FY2023  
          Exhibits FY2023  
            Remediation  
          34.1  
          34.8  
          41.5  
          42.5  
            Industrial Cleaning  
          20.1  
          19.2  
          24.9  
          24.4  
            Emergency Response  
          7.2  
          7.4  
          9.7  
          9.2  
                Total  
              61.4  
              61.4  
              76.1  
              76.1  
         Projection mix (totals match; allocation differs):  
                         ($M)  
          CIM FY2025E Rem/IC/ER  
          Exhibits FY2025E Rem/IC/ER  
            Remediation  
          56.0  
          56.5  
            Industrial Cleaning  
          29.5  
          30.2  
            Emergency Response  
          13.0  
          11.8  
                Total  
              98.5  
              98.5  
         The CIM projects Emergency Response growing ~15% annually (to $13.0M / $15.0M / $17.0M), while the Exhibits project a more conservative ramp ($11.8M / $13.0M / $15.0M). For an inherently event-driven segment, the Exhibits’ profile is more defensible; the CIM’s higher ER assumption inflates the less-predictable portion of the plan.  
        4.3 Adjusted EBITDA Bridge — The $17.1M Does Not Foot  
      The seller’s FY2024 bridge (identical in the CIM and the Exhibits):  
                        Item ($M)  
          Amount  
            Reported EBITDA  
          12.1  
            (+) Owner compensation normalization  
          1.9  
            (+) Related-party lease normalization  
          0.8  
            (+) One-time legal/regulatory costs  
          1.2  
            (+) Non-recurring equipment relocation  
          0.4  
            (+) Stock-based compensation (phantom)  
          0.3  
            (+) One-time IT implementation  
          0.1  
                Sum of adjustments  
              4.7  
                Reported + adjustments  
              16.8  
                Adjusted EBITDA (as stated)  
              17.1  
                Overstatement  
              0.3  
         The six adjustments sum to $4.7M; $12.1M + $4.7M =             $16.8M    , yet Adjusted EBITDA is stated as $17.1M in both documents. This $0.3M arithmetic error propagates directly into the headline valuation metric.             The correct stated Adjusted EBITDA is $16.8M.  
      Beyond the arithmetic error, each adjustment warrants scrutiny:  
              Owner compensation ($1.9M).         Oakes’ total comp is $2.4M; a $500K replacement CEO is assumed. The comp level and the replacement assumption are reasonable in direction but must be validated in QoE (including the         “    personal expenses allocated to the Company    ”         component). Replacement-CEO recruitment in this sector runs 6–12 months (sector memo).  
              Related-party lease ($0.8M).         CES pays Oakes $1.4M/yr for the HQ he owns personally; market rent is estimated at $600K. The add-back is only valid if the buyer can secure market-rate occupancy — which requires either purchasing the property from Oakes at fair value (additional cash at close) or renegotiating a lease with a seller who retains leverage post-close.             This add-back should be treated as conditional, not automatic.  
              Regulatory/legal ($1.2M).         The August 14, 2024 Oregon DEQ enforcement action ($875K settlement + $325K legal) is presented as fully non-recurring. Per sector benchmarks and Thornfield portfolio data, an ongoing regulatory/compliance run-rate of $200K–$500K/yr is inherent to this business model. Only the excess over run-rate should be added back:             defensible add-back ≈ $0.7M–$0.9M    , not $1.2M (a $0.3M–$0.5M reduction). GreenWorks and Allied Waste both demonstrated that         “    one-time    ”         regulatory costs recurred at ~$275K–$310K/yr.  
              Equipment relocation ($0.4M) / IT ($0.1M).         Plausibly one-time; accept pending confirmation.  
              Phantom-unit SBC ($0.3M).         Non-cash, but the units are to be             settled in cash at closing         — a real cash outflow that belongs in the equity bridge, not just an EBITDA add-back. The total cash settlement liability must be sized (the $0.3M is the annual expense, not the aggregate liability).  
        4.4 Free Cash Flow and Capital Expenditures  
                            ($M)  
          FY2021  
          FY2022  
          FY2023  
          FY2024  
            Cash from operations (CIM / Exhibits)  
          5.8 / 6.4  
          6.4 / 7.0  
          7.8 / 9.1  
          9.2 / 10.0  
            Total capex  
          3.6  
          4.1  
          5.8  
          7.2  
            Free cash flow (CIM / Exhibits)  
          2.2 / 2.8  
          2.3 / 2.9  
          2.0 / 3.3  
          2.0 / 2.8  
         Two concerns stand out.             First    , FCF has been essentially flat (~$2–3M) for four years despite revenue growing 42%, because capex and working capital have absorbed the incremental EBITDA. FCF conversion from reported EBITDA is only ~16–23%.             Second    , the CIM splits $7.2M of FY2024 capex into $3.0M maintenance (3.4% of revenue) and $4.2M growth. The maintenance figure is below the 5–7% sector floor ($4.4M–$6.1M) and, in the Exhibits, is shown             declining         as a percentage of revenue (3.9% → 3.4% → 3.0% → 2.9% → 2.7% through FY2027E) — the opposite of what a growing, aging fleet should exhibit. This pattern matches the pre-sale capex deferral Thornfield encountered at GreenWorks (stated 3.5%; actual 6.2%).             If true maintenance capex is ~$5.5M, annual FCF falls by ~$2.5M — potentially to near zero on the reported base.         A fleet age/condition assessment (fleet is ~4.5 years old, replacement value >$22M) is a top priority.  
        4.5 Working Capital and DSO  
      Net working capital of $11.8M (13.5% of revenue) is within the 10–14% sector range and reconciles between the CIM and Exhibits (though the underlying components do not — see §5).             The stated DSO of 52 days does not reconcile to the balance sheet.         AR of $16.9M against $87.3M revenue implies             ~71 days    , not 52; a 52-day DSO would require AR of only ~$12.4M. A ~71-day DSO is above the 45–55 day sector norm and above the 60-day level that can indicate collection issues or revenue-recognition timing differences. This must be independently calculated in QoE from the AR aging and trailing revenue. The Exhibits also disclose $2.7M of unbilled revenue (not separately presented in the CIM), which should be examined.  
        4.6 Management Projections  
                           ($M)  
          FY2024A  
          FY2025E  
          FY2026E  
          FY2027E  
            Revenue  
          87.3  
          98.5  
          112.0  
          124.0  
            YoY growth  
          14.7%  
          12.8%  
          13.7%  
          10.7%  
            Adjusted EBITDA (stated)  
          17.1  
          19.7  
          23.5  
          26.7  
            Adj. EBITDA margin  
          19.6%  
          20.0%  
          21.0%  
          21.5%  
            of which PFAS  
          6.2  
          14.0  
          22.0  
          27.0  
         The plan relies heavily on PFAS: $15.8M of the $24.7M incremental revenue from FY2024–FY2026E (64%) comes from a practice that is two years old and generated $6.2M in FY2024. The FY2025E PFAS jump to $14.0M (125.8% growth) is roughly 3x the 30–50% company-level rate indicated by sector data and the GreenWorks analog (where an emerging service line moderated to ~35% growth after crossing $5M). Margin expansion to 21.5% is attributed to PFAS mix shift, so PFAS shortfalls compound into both revenue and margin misses. Separately, the Exhibits’ projected income statement implies FY2025E EBITDA of ~$21.2M (EBIT $16.6M + D&A $4.6M), which is             above         the CIM’s stated Adjusted EBITDA of $19.7M — an inconsistency in the forward build that should be reconciled.  
      
         5. Cross-Document Reconciliation Issues  
      The CIM, the Customer/Backlog Report, and the Financial Exhibits contain numerous inconsistencies that must be reconciled before any number is relied upon. The most material:  
       5.1 Income Statement — CIM (Appendix A) vs. Financial Exhibits  
                           ($M, FY2024)  
          CIM Appendix A  
          Financial Exhibits  
          Difference  
            Cost of revenue  
          (57.8)  
          (58.5)  
          0.7  
            Gross profit  
          29.5  
          28.8  
          0.7  
            Gross margin  
          33.8%  
          33.0%  
          80 bps  
            SG&A (CIM) / SG&A ex-owner-comp (Exh.)  
          (14.0)  
          (12.1)  
          n/a (reclassified)  
            Total operating expenses  
          (17.4)  
          (17.7)  
          0.3  
            Operating income (EBIT)  
          7.8  
          11.1  
          3.3  
            D&A  
          (4.3)  
          (4.2)  
          0.1  
            Net income  
          5.0  
          6.7  
          1.7  
         Net income differs by $1.7M in FY2024 (and by $0.9M–$1.9M in each prior year). The Exhibits break out owner compensation ($2.4M) and the related-party lease ($1.4M) as separate expense lines and present the DEQ settlement ($875K) as a non-operating         “    other    ”         item, whereas the CIM embeds these within SG&A/other operating expenses. The two presentations do not reconcile to a common EBIT or net income.  
          Internal inconsistency in the Exhibits.         The Exhibits report Operating Income (EBIT) of $11.1M and D&A of $4.2M. Because pre-tax income reconciles as EBIT − interest − other ($11.1M − $1.2M − $0.9M = $9.0M), EBIT is confirmed to be             post-D&A    . EBITDA should therefore equal $11.1M + $4.2M =             $15.3M    , yet the Exhibits state Reported EBITDA as $12.1M — a             $3.2M unreconciled gap    . The CIM’s own presentation (EBITDA $12.1M − D&A $4.3M = EBIT $7.8M) is internally consistent, but it does not agree with the Exhibits’ EBIT of $11.1M.             The two documents disagree on EBIT by $3.3M and on the very definition/presentation of EBITDA.  
        5.2 Balance Sheet — CIM (Section VI.D / Appendix A) vs. Financial Exhibits  
                          ($M, 12/31/24)

         Notably,             net working capital reconciles         ($11.8M in both) and             net debt coincidentally reconciles         (~$15.0M in both: CIM $17.3M debt − $2.3M cash; Exhibits $19.1M debt − $4.1M cash), but the gross components differ materially. The Exhibits also show             $2.5M of goodwill/intangibles         (constant across all four years) that the CIM does not disclose — yet the CIM describes CES’s growth as entirely organic and mentions no historical acquisitions. The source of this goodwill must be explained.  
        5.3 Customer Data — CIM vs. Backlog Report  
      The top-5 revenue figures agree, but             customers #6–#10 are entirely different companies         in the two documents (same revenue amounts and percentages), and several top-5 attributes conflict:  
                            Customer  
          CIM segments  
          Backlog segments  
          CIM         “    since    ”  
          Backlog         “    since    ”  
            PNR  
          IC, Remediation

          2012  
            ODOT

          Remediation, ER

            Columbia Basin  
          IC, Remediation  
          Remediation, IC

            Meridian L&P

          2018  
         The CIM’s claim of an         “    average top-10 customer relationship of 8+ years    ”         is not supported by the Backlog Report’s start dates, which imply an average of ~6.9 years as of FY2024. The Backlog Report also references PNR’s         “    Portland and Anacortes refining facilities,    ”         whereas the CIM references only PNR’s refining complex generally.  
        5.4 Geographic Mix  
      The CIM states ~75% of FY2024 revenue is from Oregon/Washington and ~25% from Idaho/Northern California. The Backlog Report’s state-level table shows             Oregon/Washington = 82.4%         ($71.9M) and             Idaho/Northern California = 17.6%         ($15.4M). The CIM overstates the newer-market contribution by ~7 points.  
        5.5 Summary of Reconciliation Status  
                        Metric  
          Reconciles?  
            FY2024 total revenue  
          Yes  
            FY2024 segment revenue (total)  
          Yes  
            FY2024 reported EBITDA  
          Yes ($12.1M)  
            FY2024 Adjusted EBITDA  
              No — arithmetic error ($0.3M)  
            Net working capital  
          Yes ($11.8M)  
            Net debt  
          Yes (~$15.0M, coincidental)  
            Income statement detail (COGS, GP, EBIT, NI)  
              No  
            Balance sheet detail (cash, PP&E, debt, equity)  
              No  
            Cash flow detail (CFO, FCF)  
              No  
            Historical segment split (FY21–23)  
              No  
            Projection segment mix  
              No  
            Top-10 customer identities (#6–#10)  
              No  
            Customer relationship tenure  
              No  
            Geographic mix %  
              No  
            DSO (stated vs. calculated)  
              No  
         
         6. Key Risks and Diligence Priorities  
       6.1 Customer Concentration and Near-Term Contract Expirations  
      Top-10 concentration is 71.0% (top-5 52.5%), typical for the sector but meaningful.             The most urgent issue is PNR (21.4% of revenue), whose MSA expires March 31, 2025 — before the July 14 IOI deadline.         Renewal status must be confirmed immediately; a lapsed or renegotiated PNR contract is a deal-altering event. Other near-term expirations: Meridian L&P (Dec 31, 2025), and several #6–#10 contracts in 2026. Willamette Steel (5.6%) is on a month-to-month arrangement with no contractual security. Oil & Gas/Refining is 25.7% of revenue, with PNR alone representing ~83% of that sector — concentrating cyclical exposure in a single relationship.  
        6.2 PFAS Execution Risk  
      The growth thesis hinges on a two-year-old practice more than doubling in FY2025E. Key constraints: (i) no contracted PFAS backlog breakout is provided (signed vs. pipeline); (ii) PE headcount fell from three to two in January 2025 and has not been replaced, while PFAS work requires PE oversight — projected growth likely requires hiring 2–3 additional PEs in a tight market with 6–12 month recruitment timelines; (iii) larger national firms are entering PFAS with greater resources. Request the PFAS backlog breakout and management’s PE hiring plan as a first diligence step.  
        6.3 Capex Understatement / Fleet Condition  
      Stated maintenance capex (3.4% of revenue) is 150–200 bps below the sector floor and declining as a percentage of revenue. Combined with $2.5M of unexplained goodwill and a $22M+ fleet at ~4.5 years average age, this raises deferred-maintenance and hidden-acquisition concerns. A detailed fleet age/condition assessment and a 3-year maintenance capex rebuild are essential; the FCF model should be re-run at ~5.5–6.0% maintenance capex.  
        6.4 Regulatory Cost Recurrence and Compliance History  
      The DEQ enforcement action (improper storage of contaminated soils at the Portland facility) is presented as isolated. Request CES’s complete 10-year regulatory enforcement history across all four states. Oregon DEQ enforcement has been increasing; a pattern of prior actions would suggest systemic issues and a higher ongoing run-rate. Normalize the EBITDA bridge to a $300K–$500K regulatory run-rate.  
        6.5 Key-Person and Management Transition  
      Oakes (68% owner, 61) is central to strategy, customer relationships, and the real-estate arrangement. His planned 18–24 month transition creates execution risk; the GreenWorks analog saw a key project manager depart within six months of close. Janet Prewitt (VP Operations, 18 years) is a critical retention target.             Retention/rollover agreements for Prewitt and other key operators should be a condition of the IOI and a closing condition.         Confirm the feasibility and timing of recruiting a replacement CEO at the assumed $500K package.  
        6.6 Real Estate / Related-Party Arrangements  
      The HQ (8.5 acres, purpose-built environmental staging) is owned by Oakes and leased to CES at $1.4M/yr through 2029 — $0.8M above estimated market. The $0.8M EBITDA add-back is only realizable if the buyer secures market-rate occupancy, which requires either (a) purchasing the property from Oakes at fair value (estimated ~$6–8M at a ~7.5–8% cap on $600K market rent — additional cash consideration to the seller), (b) renegotiating the lease with a seller who retains leverage, or (c) relocating (operationally disruptive for a customized facility). The process letter explicitly asks bidders to address this. The IOI should state the buyer’s intended treatment and condition the lease add-back on a satisfactory resolution.  
        6.7 Earnings Quality and Adjustments  
      Collectively, the adjustments move EBITDA from $12.1M (reported, 13.9%) to $17.1M (stated, 19.6%) — a 41% uplift. After the arithmetic correction, regulatory normalization, and conditional treatment of the lease, a buyer-normalized figure is closer to             $16.0M–$16.5M (18.3%–18.9%)    . The gap between reported and adjusted is large enough that the QoE should test every adjustment against supporting documentation.  
        6.8 Sizing vs. Fund III Mandate  
      The CIM’s implied EV of $136.8M–$171.0M (and $157.6M–$197.0M forward) is at/above the upper bound of the         “    $30M–$100M enterprise value    ”         platform target cited in the sector memo, even though that memo states CES         “    fits squarely within these parameters.    ”         The deal team should confirm Fund III’s actual capacity and single-asset exposure limits for a transaction of this size (the $425M fund can likely support it, potentially with a co-invest), and reconcile the mandate language before committing to a process that may anchor on a $170M+ EV.  
      
         7. Valuation Analysis  
       7.1 Seller’s Implied Valuation  
      The CIM cites comparable environmental services transactions at 8.0x–10.0x Adjusted EBITDA and implies:  
              Trailing (FY2024):         $17.1M × 8.0x–10.0x =             $136.8M–$171.0M EV  
              Forward (FY2025E):         $19.7M × 8.0x–10.0x =             $157.6M–$197.0M EV  
        7.2 Buyer-Normalized EBITDA  
                          Adjustment to stated $17.1M  
          $M  
          Rationale  
            Arithmetic correction  
          (0.3)  
          $12.1M + $4.7M = $16.8M, not $17.1M  
            Regulatory add-back normalization  
          (0.3)–(0.5)  
          Reduce $1.2M to $0.7M–$0.9M (run-rate $300K–$500K)  
            Lease add-back (conditional)  
          0.0 to (0.8)  
          Hold only if market-rate occupancy is secured  
                Buyer-normalized Adj. EBITDA  
              ~16.0–16.5  
              18.3%–18.9% margin  
           7.3 Valuation Scenarios

                Buyer base case (normalized, risk-adjusted)

         A multiple of 8.0x–9.0x (rather than the full 8.0x–10.0x) on the normalized base reflects concentration risk, capex uncertainty, PFAS execution risk, regulatory history, and key-person/transition risk. The upper end of the range (9.0x+) should be reserved for an outcome in which the PNR contract is confirmed, PFAS backlog is contracted, capex is validated, and a credible management succession plan is in place.  
        7.4 Equity Bridge (Illustrative, Buyer Base Case)  
                         Item  
          $M  
            Enterprise value (midpoint of base case)  
          ~138.0  
            (–) Net debt (term loan + revolver − cash)  
          (15.0)  
            (–) Phantom-unit cash settlement at closing (to be sized)  
          (0.5–1.0)  
            (–) Other debt-like items / working capital normalization (to be confirmed)  
          TBD  
                Equity value to sellers (pre-real-estate)  
              ~122–123  
            (+) HQ real estate purchase from Oakes (if acquired, separate)  
          +6.0–8.0  
         The real-estate treatment is a structuring decision, not a component of enterprise value: if the property is purchased, it is additional consideration to Oakes; if leased at market, the $0.8M add-back is preserved but the buyer assumes landlord-tenant risk with the seller. Net working capital is stated at $11.8M (13.5% of revenue); a normalized NWC target and any seasonal adjustment must be set in the purchase agreement.  
      
         8. Transaction Structure and Process Considerations  
              Structure.         Seller expects a stock purchase of 100% equity, cash-free/debt-free, with normalized NWC. Existing Cascadia Commercial Bank debt ($17.3M per CIM; ~$19.1M per Exhibits — reconcile) to be refinanced/repaid at close.  
              Real estate.         State the intended treatment of the Oakes-owned HQ (purchase at fair value vs. market-rate lease) and condition the lease add-back accordingly. Request a property appraisal and the existing lease terms in the VDR.  
              Phantom equity.         Size the aggregate cash settlement liability and confirm treatment (deduction from equity proceeds). Ensure all phantom holders are identified.  
              Seller transition.         Accommodate Oakes’ 18–24 month advisory transition, but tie any earnout or rollover to (i) PNR renewal, (ii) PFAS revenue milestones, and (iii) successful CEO recruitment — not solely to time. Consider a seller note or rollover equity (~5–10%) to align Oakes on post-close performance and the real-estate outcome.  
              Earnout.         Given the PFAS projection risk, an earnout tied to FY2025–FY2026 PFAS revenue (e.g., $0.50–$1.00 per $1.00 of PFAS revenue above an agreed threshold, capped) is a reasonable mechanism to bridge the valuation gap between seller and buyer views.  
              Financing.         Indicate committed Fund III equity and anticipated leverage (sector-typical ~3.0x–4.0x EBITDA, ~$48–66M on the normalized base), with named lenders. Note the term loan matures August 2026, providing a natural refinancing window.  
              Approvals.         Confirm Fund III IC and LPAC approval thresholds and timeline; flag any antitrust/HSR considerations (low risk given size, but confirm).  
      
        9. Recommendation and Next Steps  
       9.1 Recommendation  
          Proceed to the July 14, 2025 IOI.         CES is an attractive platform that fits Fund III’s sector focus and benefits from durable regulatory tailwinds. The identified issues are diligence matters, not disqualifiers — but they justify a disciplined valuation anchored on a buyer-normalized EBITDA of ~$16.0M–$16.5M and an 8.0x–9.0x multiple, yielding an EV indication of approximately             $130M–$148M    , with structure (earnout, rollover, real-estate treatment) used to bridge to the seller’s expectations if diligence supports the upside.  
        9.2 IOI Value Guidance  
              Enterprise value indication:         ~$130M–$148M (cash-free/debt-free, normalized NWC), based on FY2024 buyer-normalized Adjusted EBITDA of ~$16.0M–$16.5M at 8.0x–9.0x.  
              Basis:         Trailing FY2024, with upside to a forward multiple contingent on PFAS backlog and PNR renewal confirmation in Phase II.  
              Structure:         Stock purchase; ~3.0x–4.0x leverage; seller rollover/earnout to bridge PFAS and transition risk; real-estate treatment to be specified.  
              Conditions:         Explicitly condition the indication on the Phase II items in §9.3.  
        9.3 Prioritized Diligence Items (Phase II)  
              PNR contract renewal status         (MSA expired March 31, 2025) — confirm in writing before IOI or as an immediate Phase II first step.  
              PFAS contracted backlog breakout         (signed contracts vs. pipeline) supporting the $14.0M FY2025E figure; management’s PE hiring plan.  
              Quality of Earnings         — reconcile the CIM vs. Exhibits income statement, balance sheet, and cash flow; validate every EBITDA adjustment; correct the $0.3M arithmetic error; independently calculate DSO from AR aging.  
              Fleet age/condition and maintenance capex rebuild         at 5–7% of revenue; re-run FCF.  
              10-year regulatory enforcement history         across all four states; set the ongoing compliance run-rate.  
              Customer reconciliation         — resolve the conflicting top-10 identities, segment tags, and relationship tenures between the CIM and Backlog Report; obtain the customer list and contract file.  
              Real estate         — appraisal, lease terms, and Oakes’ willingness to sell/renegotiate.  
              Phantom equity         — aggregate cash settlement liability and holder list.  
              Goodwill/intangibles         — explain the $2.5M on the balance sheet given the organic-growth narrative.  
              Management retention         — retention agreements for Prewitt and key operators; CEO succession plan and timeline.  
        9.4 Immediate Actions (Pre-IOI)  
          Submit written questions to Philip Tran (Greenleaf) requesting: (a) PNR renewal status, (b) PFAS backlog breakout, (c) reconciliation of the CIM and Exhibits financials, (d) DSO calculation methodology, (e) 10-year regulatory history, (f) goodwill origin.  
          Engage buyer’s counsel (Calloway, Barrett & Sims LLP) and select a QoE provider.  
          Confirm Fund III sizing/mandate and IC pre-approval to pursue at the indicated range.  
          Prepare IOI letter for submission by July 14, 2025.

children.body:
### FILE: cover-memo.docx
 
                STONEBRIDGE & CALLOWAY LLP  
              600 Congress Avenue, Suite 2800  •  Austin, TX 78701  
              PRIVILEGED & CONFIDENTIAL  —  ATTORNEY WORK PRODUCT  
              INTERNAL MEMORANDUM  
    
             TO:            Margaret “Meg” Calloway, Lead Partner; Meridian Litigation File  
             FROM:            David Arsenault, Senior Associate  
             DATE:            January 8, 2024  
             RE:            Threshold Issues for Review Before Filing Defendant’s Rule 12(b)(6) Motion to Dismiss — Arcadia Health Systems, LLC v. Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc., No. 1:23-cv-00847-CMA (W.D. Tex., Austin Division)  
       
               I. Purpose  
              This memorandum accompanies the draft Motion to Dismiss and proposed order, both due to be filed on or before the Court’s January 15, 2024 dispositive-motion deadline. The motion attacks all five counts of the First Amended Complaint on the merits and is, in my judgment, strong. This memo does not rehash the merits. Instead, it flags threshold issues—some potentially dispositive of the entire federal posture—that the team should resolve before, or contemporaneously with, filing. Each is presented with a recommendation.  
               II. Summary of Recommendations  
              •             URGENT — Subject-matter jurisdiction.            There likely is no complete diversity of citizenship. If confirmed, the Court lacks diversity jurisdiction and the case must be remanded to state court. This may supersede the 12(b)(6) motion. Recommend an immediate team meeting (see Part III).  
              •             File the 12(b)(6) motion as drafted.            The merits arguments for all five counts are strong and should be preserved by the January 15 deadline regardless of the jurisdictional question.  
              •             Attach the MSLSA, SOW-1, and CO-001–CO-004 as exhibits            and invoke the “referenced and central” doctrine so the Court may consider the contract terms at the pleading stage (see Part VI).  
              •             Brief both Delaware and Texas law            for the tort counts; the choice-of-law scope of § 12.7 is debatable, but the claims fail under either body of law (see Part V).  
              •             Caption the motion under Rule 12(b)(6) with alternative 12(c) framing            and use a 10-page reply limit (see Part IV).  
              •             Be prepared for the Court to grant leave to amend            on the Rule 9(b) pleading grounds for Counts II and III (see Part VIII).  
              •             Defer counterclaims and any third-party claim against Linden Park            until after the motion is resolved (see Part XI).  
               III. Threshold Issue #1 — Subject-Matter Jurisdiction: Likely No Complete Diversity (URGENT)  
              This is the most important issue in the file. Our own Notice of Removal asserted diversity jurisdiction under 28 U.S.C. § 1332(a), but the citizenship analysis in the Notice is incomplete and, on the current record, complete diversity does not appear to exist. Subject-matter jurisdiction cannot be waived and may be raised at any time by the Court sua sponte or by a party. Arbaugh v. Y & H Corp., 546 U.S. 500, 514 (2006); Fed. R. Civ. P. 12(h)(3).  
               A. The citizenship analysis.  
              Meridian is a Delaware corporation with its principal place of business in Austin, Texas. It is therefore a citizen of both Delaware and Texas. 28 U.S.C. § 1332(c)(1); Hertz Corp. v. Friend, 559 U.S. 77, 92–93 (2010). The Notice of Removal admits as much. (Notice of Removal ¶¶ 9–11.)  
              Arcadia is a Texas limited liability company, whose citizenship is determined by the citizenship of all of its members. Harvey v. Grey Wolf Drilling Co., 542 F.3d 1077, 1080 (5th Cir. 2008). The Amended and Restated Operating Agreement (produced in initial disclosures, Exhibit A) identifies three members: (1) Dr. Rachel Okonkwo, an individual domiciled in Texas (55%); (2) Martin Schreiber, an individual domiciled in Texas (15%); and (3) Apex Medical Ventures, LP, a Delaware limited partnership (30%). A limited partnership’s citizenship is determined by the citizenship of all of its partners. Carden v. Arkoma Assocs., 494 U.S. 185, 195–96 (1990). Apex’s partners are: (a) Apex Medical Ventures GP, Inc., a Delaware corporation with its principal place of business in Wilmington, Delaware—a citizen of Delaware; and (b) the Okonkwo Family Trust, whose trustee and primary beneficiary is Dr. Okonkwo, domiciled in Texas—a citizen of Texas.  
              On that record, Arcadia is a citizen of Texas (through Okonkwo, Schreiber, and the Trust) and of Delaware (through Apex Medical Ventures GP, Inc.). Meridian is a citizen of Texas and Delaware. The parties therefore share citizenship in both Texas and Delaware. Complete diversity does not exist. Strawbridge v. Curtiss, 7 U.S. (3 Cranch) 267 (1806).  
               B. The error in the Notice of Removal.  
              The Notice of Removal alleged only that “Plaintiff Arcadia is a citizen of the State of Texas” (Notice ¶¶ 12–14), based solely on Dr. Okonkwo’s domicile, and concluded that “Defendant’s Delaware citizenship is not shared by Plaintiff” (Notice ¶ 15). That analysis is wrong on two independent grounds: (1) it ignored Schreiber and Apex as members and the Trust as a partner of Apex, through which Arcadia is also a citizen of Delaware (via Apex GP, Inc.); and (2) it ignored that Meridian itself is a citizen of Texas (principal place of business in Austin), which is shared by Arcadia (via Okonkwo, Schreiber, and the Trust). Either defect alone destroys complete diversity.  
               C. A minor discrepancy to note.  
              The FAC (¶ 8) describes the Okonkwo Family Trust as “irrevocable,” while the Operating Agreement (Exhibit A, Note 3) describes it as a “revocable inter vivos trust” under Texas law. This discrepancy does not affect the analysis: under either characterization, the trust’s citizenship follows its trustee/beneficiary (Okonkwo, a Texas citizen), so the trust is a Texas citizen either way. We should nonetheless obtain the trust instrument and the Apex partnership agreement to confirm there are no additional partners or co-trustees that could change the analysis.  
               D. Consequences.  
              If the defect is confirmed, the Court lacks subject-matter jurisdiction and the case must be remanded to the 73rd District Court of Bexar County, Texas (where it was originally filed). A remand for lack of subject-matter jurisdiction may be ordered at any time—unlike procedural removal defects, which must be raised within 30 days under 28 U.S.C. § 1447(c). The MSLSA’s forum-selection clause (§ 12.8) would then likely require transfer of the state-court action to a court in Austin, Travis County, Texas. The 12(b)(6) motion could be mooted if the Court remands before ruling on it.  
               E. Strategic complications.  
              Meridian removed this case and invoked federal jurisdiction. There is authority that a removing defendant may be judicially estopped from later moving to remand on the basis of a diversity defect it created and invoked (the “blowing hot and cold” doctrine). That doctrine, however, does not bind the Court—which has an independent obligation to confirm jurisdiction—nor does it bind the plaintiff, who may move to remand at any time on a jurisdictional ground. So even if we are estopped from moving to remand, the case could still be remanded on the Court’s own motion or on Arcadia’s motion.  
               F. Federal vs. state court.  
              Federal court (Judge Alvarez) appears more favorable to us: rigorous Twombly/Iqbal pleading standards (which favor our 12(b)(6) motion) and a bench trial (the § 12.9 jury waiver is being enforced per the Scheduling Order). State court (Bexar County, likely transferred to Travis County under the forum-selection clause) may apply a more plaintiff-friendly pleading standard and, if the jury waiver is found unenforceable under Texas law, a jury trial. But we cannot manufacture jurisdiction: if there is no diversity, we cannot remain in federal court regardless of preference.  
               G. Recommendation.  
              (1) File the 12(b)(6) motion by January 15 to preserve our strongest merits arguments, which are strong independent of forum. (2) Immediately obtain the Apex partnership agreement and the trust instrument to confirm the citizenship analysis. (3) Convene a team meeting to decide how to handle the jurisdictional issue. The realistic options are: (a) do nothing and let the Court or Arcadia raise it (risk: an inopportune remand after we have invested in briefing); (b) file a separate notice/motion under Rule 12(h)(3) alerting the Court to the apparent defect (transparent, but inconsistent with our removal and likely to hasten remand); or (c) if we conclude state court is preferable, take a transparent approach. This is a strategic call for the lead partner and client. My preliminary view is to confirm the analysis, file the 12(b)(6) motion to preserve our arguments, and let the jurisdictional question resolve on its own timeline unless we conclude state court is preferable.  
               IV. Threshold Issue #2 — 12(b)(6) vs. 12(c) Characterization  
              The Standing Order (§ 3.4) provides that a preserved post-answer motion will be treated as a Rule 12(b)(6) motion, not a Rule 12(c) motion. The Scheduling Order, by contrast, states that the Court “will treat any such motion as a Rule 12(c) motion for judgment on the pleadings, evaluated under the same standard as a Rule 12(b)(6) motion.” The two orders are in tension. Because the governing standard is identical (Twombly/Iqbal), there is no practical difference. The draft motion is captioned under Rule 12(b)(6) (consistent with the Standing Order and our preserved defense) and pleads Rule 12(c) in the alternative. We preserved the defense in the Answer (First Affirmative Defense) and by letter of November 8, 2023, satisfying the Standing Order’s post-answer conditions. Note also a page-limit discrepancy: the Standing Order allows a 15-page reply, while the Scheduling Order caps the reply at 10 pages. We should use the 10-page limit to be safe.  
               V. Threshold Issue #3 — Choice of Law  
              Section 12.7 selects Delaware law to govern “this Agreement,” without regard to conflict-of-law principles. The scope of that clause as to non-contractual claims is debatable. Count I (breach of contract) is plainly governed by Delaware law, under which the contractual limitations are strictly enforced. The tort counts (II and III) are likely governed by Texas law under the most-significant-relationship test (the representations were made and received in Texas, both parties operate in Texas, and the injury was suffered in Texas). The draft motion briefs the tort counts under both Delaware and Texas law and demonstrates that they fail under either. The DTPA count is a question of Texas statute regardless of § 12.7. We should be prepared to address choice of law if the Court sets a hearing.  
               VI. Threshold Issue #4 — Documents Outside the Complaint  
              Many of our strongest arguments depend on the MSLSA, SOW-1, and the four Change Orders—the deemed-acceptance provision, the limited warranty, the consequential-damages waiver, the liability cap, the integration clause, and the data-migration allocation. These documents are referenced throughout the FAC and central to every claim, so the Court may consider them without converting the motion to summary judgment. Lormand, 565 F.3d at 255–57; Standing Order § 3.4. The draft motion invokes this doctrine. Before filing, we must attach the MSLSA, SOW-1, and CO-001–CO-004 as exhibits, and we must be careful not to rely on matters genuinely outside the pleadings (e.g., Poletti’s contemporaneous notes, the Schreiber due-diligence memo, the support-ticket logs), which would risk conversion. Those items are for summary judgment, not this motion. The motion instead relies on the FAC’s own allegations (e.g., ¶¶ 27, 31, 32, 45, 50, 53) plus the contract.  
               VII. Threshold Issue #5 — Jury Trial Waiver  
              Section 12.9 of the MSLSA contains a mutual jury-trial waiver, and the Scheduling Order reflects that the case will be tried to the bench. Arcadia nevertheless demanded a jury in the FAC, and our Answer demanded a jury “to the extent the Court determines the jury trial waiver … is not enforceable.” Texas law scrutinizes jury waivers (requiring knowing, voluntary, and conspicuous assent), so enforceability may be contested. This does not affect the 12(b)(6) motion, but it bears on trial posture and on the forum analysis in Part III.F. Flag for later.  
               VIII. Threshold Issue #6 — DTPA Pre-Suit Demand  
              The DTPA requires a 60-day pre-suit demand letter under Tex. Bus. & Com. Code § 17.505. The document set includes Arcadia’s DTPA pre-suit demand letter and our response, so it appears a demand was made. Because the § 17.49(f) exemption is dispositive, the pre-suit-demand issue is moot for the motion. We should nonetheless verify compliance (timing and content) in case the § 17.49(f) argument is somehow overcome, and we may add a § 17.505 argument as a further alternative ground in the reply if warranted.  
               IX. Threshold Issue #7 — Leave-to-Amend Risk  
              Courts frequently grant leave to amend after dismissing fraud claims for Rule 9(b) inadequacy. We have requested dismissal with prejudice, which is supported by grounds that cannot be cured by amendment: the § 17.49(f) exemption (Count V), the express-contract bar on unjust enrichment (Count IV), the deemed-acceptance and warranty-expiration provisions (Count I), and—as to the tort counts—the economic loss rule, the puffery doctrine, the integration clause, and the absence of justifiable reliance. These are legal bars, not pleading deficiencies. Even so, the Court may grant leave to amend on the Rule 9(b) grounds for Counts II and III. We should be prepared to oppose leave by emphasizing that the FAC already reflects Arcadia’s best shot (it is an amended complaint) and that the defects are substantive, not curable. If leave is granted, we should move to strike any repleaded tort claims that depend on extra-contractual representations barred by the integration clause.  
               X. Threshold Issue #8 — Statute of Limitations (weak)  
              Texas fraud carries a two-year limitations period subject to the discovery rule and fraudulent concealment. The pre-sale representations were made in October 2021–March 2022, and suit was filed in August 2023—likely timely. I do not recommend a limitations argument in the motion. The Answer preserved it generally; we can revisit if discovery reveals a later-discovered theory.  
               XI. Threshold Issue #9 — Damages-Cap Computation (§ 8.1)  
              Section 8.1 caps aggregate liability at the fees paid or payable during the 12 months preceding the “event giving rise to” liability. The identity of that “event” is ambiguous (the pre-sale representations, the Go-Live date, or the first post-Go-Live complaint), and the cap amount depends on Meridian’s billing records for the relevant period. This affects the alternative damages-limitation argument for Count I. We should pull the billing records and compute the cap under each candidate “event” so we are ready for the reply and for any damages briefing.  
               XII. Threshold Issue #10 — Counterclaims / Third-Party Practice  
              We have not asserted counterclaims or a third-party claim against Linden Park Consulting, LLC (Arcadia’s systems integrator, whose data-migration errors caused much of the harm Arcadia blames on us). The Scheduling Order contemplates third-party discovery as to Linden Park. Asserting a third-party claim now would complicate the 12(b)(6) motion and the remand analysis, and the deadline for joinder/amendment (December 15, 2023) has passed, so leave would be required. I recommend deferring any such claims until after the motion is resolved, and revisiting if the case survives dismissal.  
               XIII. Conclusion  
              The 12(b)(6) motion is strong and should be filed by January 15, 2024. The single most important open issue is subject-matter jurisdiction: on the current record, complete diversity likely does not exist, which could require remand and could moot the motion. I recommend (1) filing the motion to preserve our merits arguments; (2) immediately confirming the citizenship analysis with the Apex partnership agreement and trust instrument; and (3) convening a team meeting to decide how to handle the jurisdictional question and the federal-versus-state-court strategic choice. I am available to discuss at your convenience.
