              DEFENDANT MERIDIAN CLOUD SOLUTIONS, INC.’S MOTION TO DISMISS PLAINTIFF’S FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT PURSUANT TO FEDERAL RULE OF CIVIL PROCEDURE 12(b)(6) AND MEMORANDUM IN SUPPORT  
              Response Deadline: Twenty-one (21) days after service  
    
              TO THE HONORABLE CATHERINE M. ALVAREZ, UNITED STATES DISTRICT JUDGE:  
              Defendant Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc. (“Meridian”) files this Motion to Dismiss Plaintiff Arcadia Health Systems, LLC’s (“Arcadia”) First Amended Complaint (Dkt. No. 12) (the “FAC”) pursuant to Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 12(b)(6). As explained below, each of the FAC’s five counts fails as a matter of law. Meridian also files this Motion pursuant to Rule 12(c) in the alternative, recognizing that the Court’s Scheduling Order indicates it may treat a preserved post-answer motion as one for judgment on the pleadings; the governing standard is identical. The Rule 12(b)(6) defense was preserved in Meridian’s Answer (First Affirmative Defense) and by letter of November 8, 2023. In support, Meridian states:  
              1. Count V (DTPA) is barred by the statutory exemption in Texas Business & Commerce Code § 17.49(f), because the transaction involved total consideration of $17,050,000 and Arcadia is a limited liability company, not an individual.  
              2. Count IV (Unjust Enrichment) fails because an express, valid written contract—the Master Software License and Services Agreement (the “MSLSA”)—governs the same subject matter.  
              3. Count II (Fraud) fails under Rule 9(b), the economic loss rule, the puffery doctrine, the integration clause, and the absence of justifiable reliance.  
              4. Count III (Negligent Misrepresentation) fails for lack of an independent duty, under the economic loss rule, and because the damages sought are contractual, not out-of-pocket.  
              5. Count I (Breach of Contract) fails under the deemed-acceptance, limited-warranty, and exclusive-remedy provisions; in the alternative, the claimed damages are barred by the contractual limitation of liability and consequential-damages waiver.  
              Meridian respectfully requests that the Court grant this Motion and dismiss all counts of the FAC with prejudice.  
               I. INTRODUCTION  
              This is a case in which a sophisticated commercial party—represented by experienced counsel and armed with the results of its own due diligence—is attempting to use fraud, tort, and consumer-protection theories to escape the comprehensive written contract it negotiated and signed. Arcadia is a $62-million healthcare IT company that investigated Meridian’s product, received express disclaimers, and—through its own counsel—bargained over (and accepted) the very risk-allocation provisions it now seeks to avoid. (FAC ¶¶ 31–32.) The FAC repackages a disappointed commercial expectation as five separate causes of action. Each fails as a matter of law.  
              The parties’ relationship is governed by a fully integrated, Delaware-law contract that contains a comprehensive integration clause, an “as is” warranty disclaimer, a limited 90-day warranty with an exclusive remedy, a deemed-acceptance provision, a consequential-damages waiver, and an aggregate-liability cap. Arcadia was represented by Wexford Hale LLP throughout negotiations and expressly negotiated these provisions. (FAC ¶ 32.) The FAC nevertheless asks the Court to ignore that bargain and to permit Arcadia to recover $47.3 million in tort and statutory damages that the contract forecloses. The Court should decline the invitation.  
               II. RELEVANT FACTS  
              The Court may consider the MSLSA, Statement of Work #1 (“SOW-1”), and the four Change Orders on this Motion because they are referenced throughout the FAC and are central to every claim. See Lormand v. US Unwired, Inc., 565 F.3d 228, 255–57 (5th Cir. 2009); Standing Order § 3.4. The following facts are drawn from the FAC and these documents.  
              The parties and the contract. Meridian is a publicly traded enterprise-software company with approximately $380 million in annual revenue. (FAC ¶ 9.) Arcadia is a regional healthcare IT provider with approximately $62 million in annual revenue. (FAC ¶ 6.) On March 15, 2022, the parties executed the MSLSA, with a total contract value of $14,700,000, and SOW-1. (FAC ¶¶ 33–35.) The MSLSA selects Delaware law to govern “this Agreement.” (MSLSA § 12.7.)  
              Sophisticated, counseled negotiation. Arcadia engaged outside counsel to negotiate the MSLSA and “negotiate more favorable terms, including enhanced limitation of liability provisions and expanded warranty protections.” (FAC ¶ 32.) The risk-allocation provisions were thus the subject of deliberate, informed negotiation—not boilerplate that escaped notice.  
              The integration clause and warranty disclaimer. Section 12.1 provides that the MSLSA “constitutes the entire agreement between the Parties … and supersedes all prior and contemporaneous agreements, proposals, representations, and understandings, whether oral or written,” and that “[e]ach Party acknowledges that, in entering into this Agreement, it has not relied upon any statement, representation, warranty, or agreement of the other Party except as expressly set forth in this Agreement.” Section 9.4 disclaims all warranties other than the limited warranty in Section 9.1 and states that the software is provided “as is” and that Arcadia “has relied on its own due diligence and evaluation in selecting the Licensed Software.”  
              Deemed acceptance and the limited warranty. Section 5.3 gives Arcadia 30 days following deployment to conduct acceptance testing and deliver written notice of any Material Nonconformity; failure to do so results in “deemed” acceptance. (MSLSA § 5.3(a), (d).) SOW-1 fixes the Acceptance Testing Period at 30 days following the Go-Live Date. (SOW-1 § 4.2.) NexusCore went live on January 15, 2023 (FAC ¶ 45), making February 14, 2023 the deadline for a Nonconformity Notice. Arcadia did not deliver any written notice of nonconformity until Dr. Okonkwo’s email of March 8, 2023 (FAC ¶ 50)—22 days after the window closed. The Section 9.1 limited warranty runs for 90 days following Go-Live (i.e., through April 15, 2023) and is Arcadia’s sole remedy for software nonconformance: repair or replacement, or a pro-rata refund if Meridian cannot cure within 60 days. (MSLSA § 9.1(b).)  
              Data migration was Arcadia’s responsibility. SOW-1 expressly provides that Arcadia “shall be solely responsible for all data migration activities, including extraction, transformation, and loading of legacy data,” and that Meridian’s role was advisory only, with “no responsibility for the accuracy, completeness, or integrity of migrated data.” (SOW-1 § 3.2; MSLSA Ex. C § C.2.) The FAC nevertheless attributes data-migration problems to Meridian. (FAC ¶¶ 43, 72(d).)  
              Contractual limitations on liability. Section 8.2 waives all “indirect, incidental, special, consequential, punitive, or exemplary damages, including … damages for loss of profits, goodwill, use, data, or other intangible losses.” Section 8.1 caps each party’s aggregate liability at the fees paid or payable during the 12 months preceding the event giving rise to liability. Section 8.3 designates the MSLSA’s remedies as Arcadia’s “sole and exclusive remedies.”  
              Arcadia’s own due diligence and disclaimers. Arcadia’s CTO conducted “independent due diligence, including reference calls with two existing Meridian healthcare clients.” (FAC ¶ 31.) Meridian’s December 9, 2021 written proposal—acknowledged in the FAC—“contained certain general disclaimers.” (FAC ¶ 27.) Those disclaimers stated that estimated timelines and performance metrics “are provided for planning purposes only and do not constitute guarantees.”  
              The alleged pre-sale statements. The FAC identifies a handful of sales-presentation statements: that NexusCore “delivers industry-leading performance for healthcare analytics,” that clients “typically see 30-40% improvement in reporting efficiency,” and that NexusCore “will integrate seamlessly with any EHR platform.” (FAC ¶¶ 23, 25, 28, 79.) These are general claims of product superiority and forward-looking projections—the archetype of non-actionable puffery.  
               III. LEGAL STANDARD  
              To survive a Rule 12(b)(6) motion, a complaint must contain sufficient factual matter to “state a claim to relief that is plausible on its face.” Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544, 570 (2007); Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662, 678 (2009). The Court accepts all well-pleaded factual allegations as true, but “[t]hreadbare recitals of the elements of a cause of action, supported by mere conclusory statements, do not suffice.” Iqbal, 556 U.S. at 678. “Determining whether a complaint states a plausible claim … calls for the court to draw on its judicial experience and common sense,” and to consider whether an “obvious alternative explanation” accounts for the defendant’s conduct. Id. at 679, 682.  
              The Court may consider documents “referred to in the plaintiff’s complaint and central to the plaintiff’s claim” without converting the motion into one for summary judgment. Lormand, 565 F.3d at 255–57; see also Standing Order § 3.4. The MSLSA, SOW-1, and Change Orders satisfy that test. Claims sounding in fraud must additionally satisfy the particularity requirements of Rule 9(b). Benchmark Elecs., Inc. v. J.M. Huber Corp., 343 F.3d 719, 724 (5th Cir. 2003).  
               IV. ARGUMENT  
               A. Count V (DTPA) Is Barred by the Statutory Exemption in § 17.49(f).  
              The DTPA claim fails as a matter of law because the transaction falls within the statutory exemption in Texas Business & Commerce Code § 17.49(f). That section provides that the DTPA “does not apply to a cause of action arising from a transaction, a project, or a set of transactions relating to the same project, involving total consideration by the consumer of more than $500,000,” unless the consumer is an individual. Tex. Bus. & Com. Code § 17.49(f). Both conditions are met here.  
              First, Arcadia is a Texas limited liability company—not an individual. (FAC ¶ 6.) Second, the total consideration for this transaction vastly exceeds $500,000: the MSLSA alone had a contract value of $14,700,000 (FAC ¶ 34), and the four Change Orders added another $2,350,000 (FAC ¶ 46), for total consideration of $17,050,000—more than 34 times the statutory threshold. The exemption is determined by the total consideration involved in the transaction, not by the amount of damages claimed. PPG Indus., Inc. v. JMB/Houston Ctrs. Partners Ltd., 146 S.W.3d 79, 89 (Tex. App.—Houston [1st Dist.] 2004, no pet.). The § 17.49(f) exemption exists precisely to keep sophisticated commercial parties in high-value transactions from wielding the DTPA’s treble-damages remedy in ordinary commercial disputes. Id.  
              The FAC does not—and cannot—allege facts bringing this transaction within the DTPA. Arcadia is not an individual, and the consideration exceeds $500,000 by orders of magnitude. Count V must be dismissed. Even if the exemption did not apply, the claim would still fail: the alleged representations are non-actionable puffery (see Section IV.C.2); Arcadia cannot establish producing cause given its own conduct and the data-migration errors of its chosen systems integrator, see Melody Home Mfg. Co. v. Barnes, 741 S.W.2d 349, 351–52 (Tex. 1987); and the DTPA deceptive-act allegations sound in fraud and must satisfy Rule 9(b), Benchmark, 343 F.3d at 724.  
               B. Count IV (Unjust Enrichment) Fails Because an Express Contract Governs the Same Subject Matter.  
              Unjust enrichment is unavailable where a valid express contract covers the subject matter of the dispute. Fortune Prod. Co. v. Conoco, Inc., 52 S.W.3d 671, 684 (Tex. 2000); Excess Underwriters at Lloyd’s v. Frank’s Casing Crew & Rental Tools, Inc., 246 S.W.3d 42, 59 (Tex. 2008). The MSLSA is a comprehensive written agreement governing the parties’ entire software-licensing and implementation relationship—the very subject matter of this dispute. Arcadia does not contend that the MSLSA is void or unenforceable; to the contrary, Count I is premised on the existence and validity of the MSLSA. (FAC ¶ 69.) Arcadia thus simultaneously sues on the contract and seeks quasi-contractual recovery for the same subject matter—claims that are irreconcilably inconsistent. Fortune Prod., 52 S.W.3d at 684.  
              Arcadia’s only basis for unjust enrichment is that the MSLSA’s limitation-of-liability and warranty provisions prevent full recovery under the contract. (See FAC ¶ 99 (unjust enrichment pleaded “in the alternative … to the extent the MSLSA or any provision thereof is found to be unenforceable”).) But the Texas Supreme Court has squarely rejected the argument that contractual limitations on recovery create a “gap” that unjust enrichment may fill. Excess Underwriters, 246 S.W.3d at 59–60. “Equity follows the law”: where sophisticated parties have allocated risk through a negotiated contract, equity will not rewrite the bargain. Id. Count IV must be dismissed.  
               C. Count II (Fraud) Fails Under Rule 9(b), the Economic Loss Rule, the Puffery Doctrine, and the Integration Clause.  
               1. The FAC does not satisfy Rule 9(b).  
              Rule 9(b) requires a fraud plaintiff to “specify the statements contended to be fraudulent, identify the speaker, state when and where the statements were made, and explain why the statements were fraudulent.” Benchmark, 343 F.3d at 724. The FAC identifies the speaker (Poletti) and the dates of the presentations (FAC ¶¶ 22, 24), but it does not allege facts showing why the statements were false when made. Its theory is entirely retrospective: because NexusCore allegedly performed poorly after Go-Live in January 2023, the pre-sale statements made in October–November 2021 must have been false. (FAC ¶ 80.) Post-hoc product failure does not establish that a pre-sale representation was false when made. Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred Income Fund, Inc. v. TXU Corp., 565 F.3d 200, 207–08 (5th Cir. 2009).  
              The FAC’s scienter allegations are likewise deficient. Paragraphs 56–60 allege Meridian’s knowledge and concealment “on information and belief,” without any factual basis—precisely the type of conclusory averments that Rule 9(b) and Iqbal forbid. Flaherty, 565 F.3d at 207; Iqbal, 556 U.S. at 678. The FAC also “lumps” numerous statements together without distinguishing opinion, puffery, and actionable fact. Dorsey v. Portfolio Equities, Inc., 540 F.3d 333, 339–40 (5th Cir. 2008). The fraud claim does not satisfy Rule 9(b).  
               2. The alleged statements are non-actionable puffery.  
              The core representations—“industry-leading performance for healthcare analytics,” “30-40% improvement in reporting efficiency,” “seamless” EHR integration, and a “proven” and “purpose-built” platform—are general claims of product superiority and forward-looking projections. (FAC ¶¶ 23, 25, 28, 79.) Such statements are non-actionable puffery. Pizza Hut, Inc. v. Papa John’s Int’l, Inc., 227 F.3d 489, 496–97 (5th Cir. 2000); Castrol Inc. v. Pennzoil Co., 987 F.2d 939, 945 (3d Cir. 1993). Forward-looking statements about expected performance, made in a sales context, are puffery upon which a sophisticated buyer cannot reasonably rely. Presidio Enters., Inc. v. Warner Bros. Distrib. Corp., 784 F.2d 674, 679–80 (5th Cir. 1986). The December 9, 2021 written proposal—acknowledged in the FAC—expressly disclaimed that performance metrics “are provided for planning purposes only and do not constitute guarantees.” (FAC ¶ 27.)  
               3. The economic loss rule bars the fraud claim.  
              Whether governed by Delaware or Texas law, the fraud claim is barred because it arises from the contractual relationship and seeks only economic losses. Under Delaware law, the economic loss doctrine bars tort claims that seek economic losses arising from a contractual relationship absent an independent duty. Brasby v. Morris Dynamics, Inc., 947 A.2d 1042, 1049–50 (Del. 2008); Kuhn Constr., Inc. v. Diamond State Port Corp., 990 A.2d 393, 401–02 (Del. 2010). Under Texas law, the same result obtains where the losses are purely economic, relate to the subject matter of the contract, and the defendant owes no independent duty. Chapman Custom Homes, Inc. v. Dallas Plumbing Co., 445 S.W.3d 716, 718–19 (Tex. 2014); Sharyland Water Supply Corp. v. City of Alton, 354 S.W.3d 407, 415–16 (Tex. 2011). Arcadia’s alleged misrepresentations all concern the quality and performance of NexusCore—the subject of the MSLSA—and every dollar of damages sought is economic. No independent duty exists.  
               4. The integration clause and anti-reliance doctrine bar reliance on extra-contractual representations.  
              Section 12.1 is a comprehensive integration clause that supersedes “all prior and contemporaneous agreements, proposals, representations, and understandings, whether oral or written,” and provides that each party “has not relied upon any statement, representation, warranty, or agreement of the other Party except as expressly set forth in this Agreement.” Under Delaware law, a party who negotiates such a clause—represented by counsel—cannot later assert fraud based on the very extra-contractual representations the clause was designed to supersede. Eagle Indus., Inc. v. DeVilbiss Health Care, Inc., 702 A.2d 1228, 1232 (Del. 1997); SIGA Techs., Inc. v. PharmAthene, Inc., 67 A.3d 330, 344 (Del. 2013); Kuhn Constr., 990 A.2d at 401.  
               5. Reliance was not justifiable.  
              Even under Texas law, justifiable reliance is required. Hollcroft-Storms v. Pac. Mut. Life Ins. Co., 51 S.W.3d 573, 577 (Tex. 2001). The FAC’s own allegations defeat justifiable reliance: Arcadia conducted independent due diligence, including reference calls with existing Meridian clients (FAC ¶ 31); received express disclaimers in the written proposal (FAC ¶ 27); and was represented by experienced counsel who negotiated the MSLSA’s risk-allocation provisions, including the limitation of liability (FAC ¶ 32). A sophisticated party that investigates, receives disclaimers, and negotiates contractual protections cannot justifiably rely on a salesperson’s promotional puffery. Presidio, 784 F.2d at 680; Eagle Indus., 702 A.2d at 1232. Count II must be dismissed.  
               D. Count III (Negligent Misrepresentation) Fails for Lack of an Independent Duty and Because the Damages Are Contractual.  
               1. No independent duty.  
              Texas recognizes negligent misrepresentation under Restatement (Second) of Torts § 552, but liability requires a duty of care independent of any contractual obligation. McCamish, Martin, Brown & Loeffler v. F.E. Appling Interests, 991 S.W.2d 787, 791–92 (Tex. 1999). Meridian’s relationship with Arcadia is purely contractual. The MSLSA’s express warranty (§ 9.1) and warranty disclaimer (§ 9.4) define the scope of Meridian’s obligations regarding software performance. There is no fiduciary relationship, no professional duty, and no statutory duty independent of the contract. The claim is therefore barred. Id.; see also Sharyland, 354 S.W.3d at 415–16; Chapman, 445 S.W.3d at 718–19.  
               2. The damages are benefit-of-the-bargain, not out-of-pocket.  
              A negligent misrepresentation claim is available only for “out-of-pocket” losses—actual expenditures made in reliance on false information—not for benefit-of-the-bargain damages. Fed. Land Bank Ass’n of Tyler v. Sloane, 825 S.W.2d 439, 442–43 (Tex. 1992). Arcadia seeks the $14.7 million contract value, $18.4 million in lost profits, and $6.7 million in increased operating costs (FAC ¶¶ 62–64)—all benefit-of-the-bargain and consequential damages that sound in contract, not tort. Id. Arcadia’s true complaint is that NexusCore did not perform as contractually warranted—a breach of contract claim, not negligent misrepresentation.  
               3. Rule 9(b), puffery, and the reliance defenses also apply.  
              Because the negligent misrepresentation claim sounds in fraud, it must satisfy Rule 9(b). Benchmark, 343 F.3d at 724. It does not, for the reasons stated in Section IV.C.1. The alleged statements are puffery (Section IV.C.2), and reliance was not justifiable (Section IV.C.5). Count III must be dismissed.  
               E. Count I (Breach of Contract) Fails Under the Deemed-Acceptance, Warranty, and Exclusive-Remedy Provisions; In the Alternative, the Claimed Damages Are Barred by the Contractual Limitations.  
               1. Deemed acceptance bars the defective-software theory.  
              Section 5.3 of the MSLSA gives the licensee 30 days following deployment to conduct acceptance testing and deliver written notice of any Material Nonconformity; failure to do so results in “deemed” acceptance. (MSLSA § 5.3(a), (d).) SOW-1 fixes the Acceptance Testing Period at 30 days following the Go-Live Date. (SOW-1 § 4.2.) NexusCore went live on January 15, 2023 (FAC ¶ 45), making February 14, 2023 the deadline for a Nonconformity Notice. Arcadia did not deliver any written notice of nonconformity until Dr. Okonkwo’s email of March 8, 2023 (FAC ¶ 50)—22 days after the acceptance window closed. Under the deemed-acceptance provision, the software was conclusively accepted as of February 14, 2023.  
              Deemed-acceptance provisions in software contracts are enforceable and provide a bright-line rule. Simulados, Inc. v. Canton Health Mgmt. Co., 2019 WL 4573218, at *5–6 (W.D. Tex. Sept. 20, 2019) (enforcing an identical provision; oral and post-deadline complaints do not satisfy the written-notice requirement); Precision Healthcare Sols. v. Nextera Data Sys., 458 F. Supp. 3d 544, 551–52 (N.D. Tex. 2020) (deemed acceptance applies to latent as well as patent defects). The FAC’s post-Go-Live defect allegations (FAC ¶¶ 49–54) are therefore post-acceptance complaints channeled to the warranty provisions, not a basis for rejecting the software or asserting fundamental breach.  
               2. The limited warranty expired, and its remedy is exclusive.  
              Section 9.1 warrants that the software would “perform substantially in accordance with the Documentation” for 90 days following Go-Live—a period that expired on April 15, 2023. The sole and exclusive remedy for any breach of that warranty is repair or replacement, or, if Meridian cannot cure within 60 days, a pro-rata refund of pre-paid license fees. (MSLSA § 9.1(b).) Section 8.3 designates the MSLSA’s remedies as Arcadia’s “sole and exclusive remedies.” The FAC does not allege that Arcadia invoked the warranty process or that Meridian failed to cure within the contractual period; to the contrary, the FAC acknowledges that Meridian released software patches on May 2 and June 19, 2023. (FAC ¶ 53.) The breach-of-contract claim, to the extent premised on software defects, is limited to the expired warranty’s exclusive remedy.  
               3. The delay theory is attributable to Arcadia under § 3.5.  
              To the extent Count I rests on the 137-day Go-Live delay (FAC ¶¶ 45, 72(b)), Section 3.5 provides that the project schedule “shall be adjusted on a day-for-day basis” for delays caused by the licensee’s failure to fulfill its responsibilities, and that Meridian “shall not be liable for any failure to meet the Target Go-Live Date … to the extent such failure is caused by Licensee’s breach.” The FAC itself acknowledges that the parties executed four Change Orders to address evolving scope (FAC ¶¶ 46–47)—each mutually executed under § 5.4. The FAC’s conclusory attribution of the entire delay to Meridian (FAC ¶ 72) is not plausible. See Iqbal, 556 U.S. at 682.  
               4. In the alternative, the claimed damages are barred by §§ 8.1 and 8.2.  
              Even if any breach-of-contract claim survives, the FAC’s $47.3 million damages demand is barred as a matter of law. Section 8.2 waives all “indirect, incidental, special, consequential, punitive, or exemplary damages, including … damages for loss of profits, goodwill, use, data, or other intangible losses.” The $18.4 million lost-profits claim (FAC ¶ 63), the $6.7 million increased-operating-costs claim (FAC ¶ 64), the $5 million reputational-harm claim (FAC ¶ 65), and any punitive damages are consequential damages expressly waived. Section 8.1 caps each party’s aggregate liability at the fees paid or payable during the 12 months preceding the event giving rise to liability. Limitation-of-liability and consequential-damages-waiver provisions in negotiated commercial software agreements between sophisticated parties are enforceable. Dresser-Rand Co. v. Virtual Automation, Inc., 361 F.3d 831, 838–40 (5th Cir. 2004); Kana Software, Inc. v. Sealand Tech., Inc., 178 A.3d 1045, 1058–60 (Del. Ch. 2017). Arcadia was represented by counsel and unsuccessfully sought to modify these very provisions (FAC ¶ 32)—confirming that the risk allocation was deliberate and informed. Count I must be dismissed, or in the alternative, the FAC’s damages demands must be stricken to the extent they exceed the contractual limitations.  
               V. CONCLUSION  
              For the foregoing reasons, Meridian respectfully requests that the Court grant this Motion and dismiss all counts of the First Amended Complaint with prejudice, and award Meridian such other and further relief, including its reasonable attorneys’ fees and costs, as the Court deems just and proper.  
    
               CERTIFICATE OF CONFERENCE  
              Pursuant to Section 3.1 of the Court’s Standing Order for Civil Cases, counsel for Meridian conferred in good faith with counsel for Arcadia by telephone on January 4, 2024, regarding the relief sought in this Motion. Arcadia’s counsel opposes the Motion in its entirety and declined to voluntarily narrow or dismiss any claim. The parties were unable to reach agreement.  
             /s/ Margaret “Meg” Calloway  
             Margaret “Meg” Calloway  
               CERTIFICATE OF SERVICE  
              I hereby certify that on January 12, 2024, I electronically filed the foregoing Defendant Meridian Cloud Solutions, Inc.’s Motion to Dismiss Plaintiff’s First Amended Complaint Pursuant to Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 12(b)(6) and Memorandum in Support with the Clerk of Court using the Court’s CM/ECF system, which will send notification of such filing to all counsel of record, including:

children.name: test_evict.py
children.body:
import sys, json, importlib.util
# Load the eviction helpers directly from agent_loop without importing harness deps.
import types
# Stub the harness.adapters.base and harness.tools imports.
mod = types.ModuleType("harness"); sys.modules["harness"]=mod
adp = types.ModuleType("harness.adapters"); sys.modules["harness.adapters"]=adp
base = types.ModuleType("harness.adapters.base"); base.ModelAdapter=object; base.ModelResponse=object
sys.modules["harness.adapters.base"]=base
tools = types.ModuleType("harness.tools"); tools.ToolExecutor=object; tools.get_all_tool_definitions=lambda: []
sys.modules["harness.tools"]=tools

spec = importlib.util.spec_from_file_location("agent_loop", "harness/agent_loop.py")
al = importlib.util.module_from_spec(spec); spec.loader.exec_module(al)

BIG = "X"*60000  # ~15k tokens, a "document"
SMALL = "ok"

# ---- Anthropic-style: one user message per turn, content = list of blocks ----
def anth_results(pairs):
    return [{"role":"user","content":[{"type":"tool_result","tool_use_id":tid,"content":res} for tid,res in pairs]}]

# ---- OpenAI-style: one message per tool result ----
def oai_results(pairs):
    return [{"role":"tool","tool_call_id":tid,"content":res} for tid,res in pairs]

for style,builder in [("anthropic",anth_results),("openai",oai_results)]:
    messages=[{"role":"system","content":"sys"},{"role":"user","content":"task"}]
    tool_log=[]
    # turn 2: read big doc + small
    class TC:
        def __init__(s,n,i,a): s.name=n; s.id=i; s.arguments=a
    rms=builder([("t1",BIG),("t2",SMALL)])
    messages.extend(rms)
    tool_log.append({"turn":2,"messages":rms,"items":[
        {"name":"read","args":{"file_path":"documents/big.docx"},"full":BIG,"evicted":False},
        {"name":"read","args":{"file_path":"documents/s.txt"},"full":SMALL,"evicted":False}]})
    # turn 3 bash big
    rms2=builder([("t3",BIG)])
    messages.extend(rms2)
    tool_log.append({"turn":3,"messages":rms2,"items":[
        {"name":"bash","args":{"command":"cat documents/big2"},"full":BIG,"evicted":False}]})

    def total_chars(ms): return len(json.dumps(ms))
    before=total_chars(messages)
    # at turn 5: window=4 -> nothing aged yet (5-2=3 < 4). expect no change.
    al._evict_aged_results(tool_log,5)
    mid=total_chars(messages)
    # at turn 7: 7-2=5>=4 evict turn2 big; 7-3=4>=4 evict turn3 big.
    al._evict_aged_results(tool_log,7)
    after=total_chars(messages)
    # idempotent re-run
    al._evict_aged_results(tool_log,8)
    after2=total_chars(messages)
    blob=json.dumps(messages)
    print(f"[{style}] before={before:,} turn5={mid:,} turn7={after:,} turn8={after2:,}")
    assert mid==before, "should not evict inside window"
    assert after < before*0.2, "should have evicted both big docs"
    assert after2==after, "idempotent"
    assert "context-saver" in blob, "stub present"
    assert BIG not in blob, "big doc fully removed"
    assert SMALL in blob, "small result kept"
    assert "documents/big.docx" in blob, "read handle has filepath"
    assert "cat documents/big2" in blob, "bash handle has command"
    # task + system preserved
    assert any(m.get("role")=="system" for m in messages) and any(m.get("content")=="task" for m in messages)
    print(f"[{style}] OK — evicted to {after/before:.1%} of original, handles + task preserved")
print("ALL TESTS PASSED")

children.name: test_evict2.py
children.body:
import sys, json, importlib.util, types
mod = types.ModuleType("harness"); sys.modules["harness"]=mod
adp = types.ModuleType("harness.adapters"); sys.modules["harness.adapters"]=adp
base = types.ModuleType("harness.adapters.base"); base.ModelAdapter=object; base.ModelResponse=object
sys.modules["harness.adapters.base"]=base
tools = types.ModuleType("harness.tools"); tools.ToolExecutor=object; tools.get_all_tool_definitions=lambda: []
sys.modules["harness.tools"]=tools
spec = importlib.util.spec_from_file_location("agent_loop", "harness/agent_loop.py")
al = importlib.util.module_from_spec(spec); spec.loader.exec_module(al)
print("window=",al._RECENT_WINDOW,"minchars=",al._MIN_EVICT_CHARS)
BIG="X"*60000
def anth(pairs): return [{"role":"user","content":[{"type":"tool_result","tool_use_id":t,"content":r} for t,r in pairs]}]

# --- stickiness test ---
messages=[{"role":"system","content":"sys"},{"role":"user","content":"task"}]
tool_log=[]; evicted=set(); sticky=set()
def rec(turn,name,fp,full,tid):
    rms=anth([(tid,full)]); messages.extend(rms)
    # mimic loop: mark sticky if re-read of evicted
    if name=="read" and fp in evicted: sticky.add(fp)
    tool_log.append({"turn":turn,"messages":rms,"items":[{"name":name,"args":{"file_path":fp},"full":full,"evicted":False}]})

rec(2,"read","documents/agreement.docx",BIG,"a")   # first read
al._evict_aged_results(tool_log,7,sticky,evicted)   # turn7: age=5>=5 -> evict, evicted={agreement}
assert "context-saver" in json.dumps(messages) and BIG not in json.dumps(messages), "first read evicted"
assert "documents/agreement.docx" in evicted
# agent re-reads at turn 8 (saw stub)
rec(8,"read","documents/agreement.docx",BIG,"b")
assert "documents/agreement.docx" in sticky, "re-read -> sticky"
# now age out the re-read at turn 14 (14-8=6>=5) -> must STAY (sticky)
al._evict_aged_results(tool_log,14,sticky,evicted)
blob=json.dumps(messages)
assert blob.count(BIG)==1, f"sticky re-read must stay resident, found {blob.count(BIG)} copies"
print("STICKY OK: doc evicted once, re-read stays resident; no churn")

# --- non-sticky doc still evicts (read once, never re-read) ---
messages2=[{"role":"system","content":"s"}]; tl2=[]; ev2=set(); st2=set()
rms=anth([("z",BIG)]); messages2.extend(rms)
tl2.append({"turn":2,"messages":rms,"items":[{"name":"read","args":{"file_path":"documents/once.docx"},"full":BIG,"evicted":False}]})
al._evict_aged_results(tl2,9,st2,ev2)
assert BIG not in json.dumps(messages2), "read-once doc must evict"
print("EVICT-ONCE OK: read-once doc still evicted (big win preserved)")
print("ALL PASS")

children.name: test_evict3.py
children.body:
import sys, json, importlib.util, types
for m,attrs in [("harness",{}),("harness.adapters",{}),
                ("harness.adapters.base",{"ModelAdapter":object,"ModelResponse":object}),
                ("harness.tools",{"ToolExecutor":object,"get_all_tool_definitions":(lambda:[])})]:
    mod=types.ModuleType(m); [setattr(mod,k,v) for k,v in attrs.items()]; sys.modules[m]=mod
spec=importlib.util.spec_from_file_location("agent_loop","harness/agent_loop.py")
al=importlib.util.module_from_spec(spec); spec.loader.exec_module(al)
print("window",al._RECENT_WINDOW,"minchars",al._MIN_EVICT_CHARS,"sizegate",al._SIZE_GATE_CHARS)
BIG="X"*120000  # 120k chars, one big doc
def anth(pairs): return [{"role":"user","content":[{"type":"tool_result","tool_use_id":t,"content":r} for t,r in pairs]}]

# --- 1. SIZE GATE: small task (resident < gate) -> NO eviction ---
msgs=[{"role":"system","content":"s"}]; tl=[]; ev=set(); st=set()
small=anth([("a","Y"*100000)])  # 100k < 500k gate
msgs.extend(small); tl.append({"turn":2,"messages":small,"items":[{"name":"read","args":{"file_path":"a.docx"},"full":"Y"*100000,"evicted":False}]})
al._evict_aged_results(tl,20,st,ev)  # way past window, but under gate
assert "Y"*100000 in json.dumps(msgs), "under size gate must NOT evict"
print("SIZE-GATE OK: small task untouched (bare behavior, zero accuracy risk)")

# --- 2. big task (resident > gate) -> evicts aged ---
msgs=[{"role":"system","content":"s"}]; tl=[]; ev=set(); st=set()
for i in range(5):  # 5*120k = 600k > 500k gate
    r=anth([(f"d{i}",BIG)]); msgs.extend(r)
    tl.append({"turn":2+i,"messages":r,"items":[{"name":"read","args":{"file_path":f"doc{i}.docx"},"full":BIG,"evicted":False}]})
al._evict_aged_results(tl,2+4+8,st,ev)  # turn 14: docs at turns2-6 aged (>=7)
blob=json.dumps(msgs)
assert "context-saver" in blob and blob.count(BIG)<=1, f"over gate should evict aged docs, {blob.count(BIG)} big left"
assert len(ev)>=4, f"evicted keys tracked: {ev}"
print(f"OVER-GATE OK: evicted aged docs, evicted_keys={sorted(ev)}")

# --- 3. BASENAME stickiness across path spellings ---
msgs=[{"role":"system","content":"s"}]; tl=[]; ev=set(); st=set()
# fill over gate with filler so eviction runs
fr=anth([("f","Z"*500000)]); msgs.extend(fr); tl.append({"turn":1,"messages":fr,"items":[{"name":"bash","args":{"command":"cat big"},"full":"Z"*500000,"evicted":False}]})
r1=anth([("x",BIG)]); msgs.extend(r1); tl.append({"turn":2,"messages":r1,"items":[{"name":"read","args":{"file_path":"documents/agreement.docx"},"full":BIG,"evicted":False}]})
al._evict_aged_results(tl,12,st,ev)  # evict agreement -> ev has basename
assert "agreement.docx" in ev, f"basename tracked: {ev}"
# agent re-reads with DIFFERENT spelling (absolute path) -> should become sticky
key=al._read_key({"file_path":"/workspace/documents/agreement.docx"})
assert key=="agreement.docx" and key in ev, "different spelling maps to same basename"
st.add(key)  # mimic loop marking sticky
r2=anth([("y",BIG)]); msgs.extend(r2); tl.append({"turn":13,"messages":r2,"items":[{"name":"read","args":{"file_path":"/workspace/documents/agreement.docx"},"full":BIG,"evicted":False}]})
al._evict_aged_results(tl,25,st,ev)  # turn25: re-read aged, but sticky -> stays
assert json.dumps(msgs).count(BIG)==1, "sticky (basename) re-read must stay resident across spellings"
print("BASENAME-STICKY OK: re-read under any path spelling pins the doc, no churn")
print("ALL PASS")

steps.result:
# CLAUDE.md — Mutator (research agent) · EFFICIENCY mission

You are reading your own manual. It is auto-loaded every iteration. Read it, then act.

## 1. Your single objective — make the Harvey agent's rollouts cheaper, at no loss of accuracy

You are the **mutator**: a research agent that edits the **Harvey agent's harness** (its
`system_prompt.md`, `tools.py`, `agent_loop.py`). You are **not** the Harvey agent — you never do the
legal work yourself; you change the harness so the agent runs **better**, on **any** matter of its
kind, not just the ones you see.

This run has **exactly one goal**: make the Harvey agent's rollouts **more token-efficient** — fewer
tokens consumed per task, above all the **input tokens it carries across turns** — **without
degrading accuracy.**

- **Accuracy is a HARD GATE.** A committed edit must keep held-out accuracy **at or above bare** (within
  noise). Improving accuracy is a welcome bonus, but it is **not** the objective and you must not chase
  it. If an edit saves tokens but drops accuracy below the gate, it is **rejected** — no exceptions.
- **Cost is the objective.** Among edits that hold accuracy, the best one is the one that **cuts the
  most cost.** You are optimizing *cost at fixed-or-better quality.*

Everything else a harness could improve is **out of scope this run.** Do not add accuracy features,
do not diversify across "levers," do not polish outputs. One dimension: cost. That focus is the point.

## 2. Where the cost is — the carried-input problem

The Harvey agent is extremely **input-bound, and its input is cumulative.** It reads large documents,
and **everything it has read rides along on every later turn's input.** Output is tiny by comparison.
The dominant cost is therefore **re-sending the carried context, turn after turn** — not the reading
itself, and not the output.

How lopsided this is (read your own rollouts for the real numbers): a single task can run **millions
of input tokens against tens of thousands of output tokens** — input dwarfs output by ~50×, and most
of that input is the same documents carried forward again and again. **That re-carried context is the
thing to attack.**

**Localize the cost before you edit.** For each rollout, the metrics expose
`input_tokens`, `output_tokens`, `total_tokens`, `cache_read_input_tokens`, `turn_count`,
`documents_read`. Read them: *which* tasks balloon, *how many* turns, *how* the carried context grows
across turns. Target the real cost you find, not a guess. (Build yourself a small analysis tool over
these metrics in `.claude/tools/` — §5 — it'll pay off every iteration.)

## 3. The action space — `harness/**`, all aimed at cost

You edit the same three files; weights are not in scope. Every edit must serve cost reduction:

| file | the cost lever it controls |
|---|---|
| `agent_loop.py` (control flow) | **the highest-leverage place** — *how the agent carries what it has read across turns*: compaction, an external findings store, dropping/re-fetching old raw outputs, append-only context, sub-agents. |
| `tools.py` (the agent's tools) | how a tool **returns** its result (a distilled handle vs. dumping raw bytes into context); processing a document and returning just the answer instead of pulling it in whole. |
| `system_prompt.md` (prose) | can *ask* the agent to be economical, but prose is advice it can skip — the durable cost wins are **enforced** in the loop/tools, not requested in prose. |

**Directions recent long-context work points to for *this* problem.** These are **inspiration, not a
checklist** — your rollout evidence decides which fits. All of them shrink the **carried** context
while keeping the facts:

1	"""The agent loop — model calls tools until it finishes or hits max turns.
2	
3	This is the core of the harness. It's deliberately simple: the model does
4	the thinking, the loop just shuttles messages back and forth.
5	
6	The agent finishes when it stops making tool calls (no explicit `finish`
7	tool). The agent loop ends on:
8	  1. No tool calls returned — the model has nothing more to do
9	  2. Max turns reached
10	"""
11	
12	import time
13	import json
14	from pathlib import Path
15	
16	from harness.adapters.base import ModelAdapter, ModelResponse
17	from harness.tools import ToolExecutor, get_all_tool_definitions
18

31	    Args:
32	        adapter: The model adapter (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, xAI).

34	        user_prompt: The first user message — the task assignment.
35	        tool_executor: Configured tool executor with documents and output dirs.
36	        tools: Tool definitions to use. Defaults to standard 6 tools if not provided.
37	        max_turns: Maximum number of loop iterations.
38	        transcript_path: Optional path to write transcript JSONL.
39	
40	    Returns:
41	        Dict with run results: messages, metrics, timing.
42	    """
43	    messages = [
44	        adapter.make_system_message(system_prompt),
45	        adapter.make_user_message(user_prompt),
46	    ]
47	    if tools is None:
48	        tools = get_all_tool_definitions()
49	
50	    total_input_tokens = 0
51	    total_output_tokens = 0

57	    transcript_file = None

62	        print(json.dumps({"turn": 0, "role": "user", "text": user_prompt}), file=transcript_file)
63	        transcript_file.flush()
64	
65	    context_overflow = False

1	You are an AI agent executing a task provided by the user within a workspace.
2	
3	## Workspace layout
4	
5	Everything you work with lives under one workspace root. **`bash` starts in
6	`$WORKSPACE_DIR`**, so `bash ls` shows you the whole layout at a glance:
7	`documents/  output/  skills/` plus any scratch files you create.
8	
9	- **`$WORKSPACE_DIR`** — your working area, default `bash` cwd. Use it for
10	  notes, intermediate files, and skill output. Skill scripts live at
11	  `$WORKSPACE_DIR/skills/<name>/scripts/`.
12	- **`$DOCUMENTS_DIR`** (`$WORKSPACE_DIR/documents`) — task documents.
13	  Read-only.
14	- **`$OUTPUT_DIR`** (`$WORKSPACE_DIR/output`) — deliverables. The harness
15	  routes relative `write` and `edit` paths here automatically.
16	- **Task configuration** (`task.json`) — contains the task definition and the
17	  grading rubric. Do not read, search, or reference it. Doing so will be
18	  flagged as a rule violation and automatically fail the task.
19	
20	## Tool conventions
21	
22	- Use `read` to consume input files (handles .docx, .xlsx, .pptx, .pdf, and
23	  plain text).
24	- Use the file-type skill manuals below to produce binary deliverables

26	- Use `write` only for plain markdown — typically a `response.md`
27	  summarizing your work.
28	- Use `edit` for incremental refinement of a file you have already created.
29	
30	The skill manuals immediately below describe how to work with specific file
31	formats. Read them before tackling the task.

1	# Harvey harness (bare — this is what you optimize)
2	
3	This directory holds the **editable surface of the bare Harvey harness** — no skill, no
4	playbook. It is seeded from the canonical harness (`baseline-submodules/harvey-labs`, the same
5	checkout that produced the bare baseline) before each run, so three files appear here:
6	
7	| File | What it is | What an edit does |
8	|---|---|---|
9	| `system_prompt.md` | The agent's **system-prompt preamble** (workspace layout + tool conventions), prepended to every run; the file-format skill manuals (.docx/.xlsx/.pptx) are appended after it. | The prose lever — add standing method/guidance (this is where a "playbook" would now live, but you decide if prose is the highest-leverage change). |
10	| `tools.py` | The **tools the Harvey agent calls** during a run — today 6 shell tools (`bash read write edit glob grep`) over a sandbox. | Add a new action the agent can take, or sharpen a tool. |
11	| `agent_loop.py` | The **control flow**: call the model, run its tool calls, repeat until it stops or hits `max_turns`. Today a flat loop. | Add structure — compaction (a running `memo.md`), a plan→execute pass, a validate→revise step. |
12	
13	You **start from bare and discover what to add** — there is no seed skill. Edit these files in
14	place; at rollout time the full harness is reconstituted from the submodule (the model adapters,
15	`run.py`, the sandbox, the tasks) and **your edited files are overlaid on top**, so a change here
16	takes effect in the run and is what gets scored.
17	
18	You do **not** run this harness code in your own container — `run_harvey_agent` executes it
19	host-side on a real task and returns the deliverable + score. Edit the files; validate with an
20	experiment.
21	
22	**Routing rule:** a tool the **Harvey agent** uses during its run → here (`harness/tools.py`). A
23	tool that helps **you** optimize (run an experiment, grade a draft, check budget) →
24	`.claude/tools/`. Only `harness/**` is scored.
25

1	"""Tool definitions and execution for the agent evaluation harness.
2	
3	Six tools (closed-universe — no web access):
4	  bash, read, write, edit, glob, grep
5	
6	The agent finishes when it stops making tool calls (no explicit `finish`
7	tool).
8	
9	Architecture:
10	  ToolExecutor is a thin layer over a `Sandbox` (sandbox/ package). All
11	  filesystem and shell operations route through `sandbox.exec`,
12	  `sandbox.read_file`, `sandbox.write_file`, and `sandbox.list_files`.
13	  Document parsing (.docx → markdown, .pdf → text, etc.) lives here on the
14	  host since it needs Python libraries that aren't worth shipping into the
15	  sandbox image.
16	
17	  The agent sees a single sandbox-relative workspace root:
18	      /workspace                    (read-write) — agent's working area, default cwd
19	      /workspace/documents          (read-only)  — task documents
20	      /workspace/output             (read-write) — deliverables
21	  Relative paths are resolved against /workspace, then /workspace/documents,
22	  then /workspace/output — matching the legacy "check scratch, then documents"
23	  lookup order.

=== run_harvey_agent.md ===
# Tool — `run_harvey_agent`

> The mutator invokes this from its shell to run a BATCH of Harvey rollouts concurrently, for triage.

## Purpose

Run the **frozen Harvey agent** on **one or many task ids** (a **batch**, run **concurrently**),
using your **current `harness/`**: the broker overlays your edited `system_prompt.md` / `tools.py` /
`agent_loop.py` onto the bare harvey-labs checkout and runs that (exactly what the standard eval
would run), producing a deliverable + trajectory **per task** you can inspect and proxy-grade. This
is how you *try* a harness edit before the canonical eval sees it — and how you buy **breadth**:
test one edit across several tasks in a **single call** (they run in parallel) instead of one slow
serial rollout at a time.

The Harvey agent is **frozen** here: this tool does not change the agent, the model, or the
endpoint — it executes the harness as-is. Your edits to `harness/` are what change the output.

## Interface

- **Input:** one or more `task_id`s from your **TRAIN access set** (the matters under `rollouts/` /
  the train manifest), space-separated: `run_harvey_agent <task_id> [task_id2 ...]`. The batch runs
  concurrently (capped at a few at a time). Held-out **val** ids are **not available**; a batch
  containing **any** non-train id is **refused whole**.
- **ASYNC — returns immediately, runs in the background.** A batch takes ~15-20 min (each rollout
  ~9-10 min), longer than the shell's 10-minute cap, so the command **detaches a worker that survives
  the cap** and returns at once. It prints a **done-marker path** `scratch/runs/.batch_<id>.done`
  (and a live log). **Workflow: launch → poll `test -f <marker>` → `cat` the marker for the summary →
  THEN `proxy_eval`.** The deliverables are NOT ready the instant it returns — polling first is
  required, and it's what prevents the old failure (client killed at 10 min, broker keeps charging,
  deliverables lost). Don't re-launch the same batch while it's still running — you'd pay twice.
- **Output (all under `scratch/`):** one dir per task — `scratch/runs/<task_id>/deliverable.md`
  (pass it to `proxy_eval`) **and `scratch/runs/<task_id>/turns.txt`** (the rollout's turn count) for
  **`check_runaway`** (the runaway gate) — both written when the batch completes. The done-marker
  holds the per-task summary (`wrote ... turns=...`, `skipped_for_budget`, or `OVER_BUDGET`); a task
  that errors is reported there but doesn't sink the rest of the batch.
- Writes **nothing** outside `scratch/`.

## Cost

≈ **~$1 per task** (one Harvey-agent rollout) × the number of tasks in the batch. **Metered** — see
`budget.md`; a batch is charged per task only up to the remaining cap (the excess is skipped, not
charged). The unit cost is surfaced by the `budget` tool so you can size your batch.

## Guardrails

- **Counts against the budget.** Charged per task, pre-spend. If the **whole** batch is over budget
  the call returns OVER_BUDGET (your "commit now" cue); a partially-over batch runs what fits and
  returns the rest as `skipped_for_budget`. Check `budget` to size your batch. The budget is there to
  be **used**: confirm a candidate across **several distinct train tasks in one batch** (and against
  bare on the same tasks), not a single rollout — breadth across different matters is what shows the
  edit *generalizes* to tasks you can't see, and it's the main thing to spend the budget on
  (CLAUDE.md §3). A near-empty ledger means you under-experimented.
- **Never writes to `rollouts/`.** `rollouts/` is the canonical standard-eval path, owned by the
  outer driver. This tool is private triage; its outputs are ephemeral `scratch/` artifacts and
  **must not feed selection**.
- **Train-only — the val set is unr

steps.text:
Budget is healthy ($90 metered cap, untouched). I now have a clear diagnosis:

**The cost structure:** `input_tokens` = Σ over turns of the full re-sent context. The agent reads all big docs in the first ~6 turns, then spends 15–110 turns drafting via `bash`/`write` (tiny tool results) while **re-carrying every raw document on every one of those turns**. That resident raw-doc bulk, multiplied across drafting turns, is where millions of tokens go.

**The mechanism:** in `agent_loop.py`, evict large, *aged* raw tool outputs (read/bash) from the carried context — replacing each with a compact **re-read handle** stub — while keeping a generous recent window full. The agent read each doc in full (accuracy preserved at read time); it just stops re-carrying the bytes once they've aged out, and can re-read on demand. Let me first build my analysis tool (compounds), then the edit.

The key cost model (verified): metrics.input_tokens == sum over turns of the

turn, so big early reads dominate. This tool surfaces that.

"""cost_rank — localize the carried-input cost across TRAIN rollouts.

Reads rollouts/**/metrics.json and (optionally) transcript.jsonl to rank tasks
by total input_tokens and to show *where* the carried context balloons.

Usage:
  cost_rank                 # rank all tasks by input_tokens (one line each)
  cost_rank <task_substr>   # per-turn breakdown for the first matching task:
                            #   per-turn prompt size, tool results, and an
                            #   estimate of how much "resident raw read/bash
                            #   output" is being re-carried each turn.

The key cost model (verified): metrics.input_tokens == sum over turns of the
per-turn prompt size. Context grows as docs are read and is re-sent every later
turn, so big early reads dominate. This tool surfaces that.
"""
import json, sys, glob, os

ROOT = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(__file__), "..", "..", "rollouts")
ROOT = os.path.abspath(ROOT)


def rank_all():
    rows = []
    for mp in glob.glob(os.path.join(ROOT, "*", "iter0", "metrics.json")):
        task = mp.split("/")[-3]
        try:
            m = json.load(open(mp))
        except Exception:
            continue
        it = m.get("input_tokens", 0)
        ot = m.get("output_tokens", 0)
        tc = m.get("turn_count", 0)
        dr = m.get("documents_read", 0)
        # crude: input per turn ~ avg resident context; ratio in/out shows how
        # input-bound the task is.
        rows.append((it, ot, tc, dr, task))
    rows.sort(reverse=True)
    tot = sum(r[0] for r in rows)
    print(f"{'input_tok':>12} {'out_tok':>9} {'turns':>5} {'docs':>4} {'in/out':>6}  task")
    for it, ot, tc, dr, task in rows:
        ratio = (it / ot) if ot else 0
        print(f"{it:>12,} {ot:>9,} {tc:>5} {dr:>4} {ratio:>6.0f}  {task[:64]}")
    print(f"\nTOTAL input_tokens across {len(rows)} tasks: {tot:,}  (mean {tot//max(1,len(rows)):,})")


def breakdown(substr):
    cand = [d for d in glob.glob(os.path.join(ROOT, "*", "iter0"))
            if substr in d]
    if not cand:
        print(f"no task dir matching '{substr}'")
        return
    tdir = cand[0]
    task = tdir.split("/")[-2]
    tpath = os.path.join(tdir, "transcript.jsonl")
    print(f"== {task} ==")
    # Track running estimate of resident "raw output" bytes (read/bash results).
    resident_chars = 0
    cum_in = 0
    print(f"{'turn':>4} {'in_tok':>9} {'cum_in':>11} {'tools':<22} {'resultlen':>10}")
    cur_turn = None
    pending = []  # (name, reslen) within a turn
    for line in open(tpath):
        d = json.loads(line)
        role = d.get("role")
        if role == "assistant":
            it = d.get("input_tokens") or 0
            cum_in += it
            tcs = d.get("tool_calls") or []
            names = ",".join(t["name"] for t in tcs)
            print(f"{d.get('turn'):>4} {it:>9,} {cum_in:>11,} {names:<22}")
        elif role == "tool":
            rl = len(d.get("result") or "")
            nm = d.get("tool_name", "")
            if rl > 2000:
                print(f"{'':>4} {'':>9} {'':>11} {'  -> '+nm:<22} {rl:>10,}")
    print(f"\ntotal input_tokens (sum per turn) = {cum_in:,}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    if len(sys.argv) > 1:
        breakdown(sys.argv[1])
    else:
        rank_all()

  1. No tool calls returned — the model has nothing more to do

The agent is input-bound: every tool result it has ever seen rides along on
every later turn's prompt, so the same large raw documents are re-sent turn

millions of input tokens vs. tens of thousands of output).

This loop evicts *aged, large* raw tool outputs (`read`/`bash`) from the
carried message history, replacing each with a compact **re-read handle**: a
one-line stub naming the tool + file/command and telling the agent how to
fetch the bytes back on demand. Accuracy is preserved because the document was
already read **in full** when it was current (facts in late schedules/tables
were seen); we only stop *re-carrying* the raw bytes once they have aged out of
the recent working window. If the agent needs the exact text again it re-reads
— a fresh, full result re-enters context.

  - Only outputs older than RECENT_WINDOW turns are eligible (the agent has had
    several turns with the doc resident to extract what it needs).
  - Only outputs larger than MIN_EVICT_CHARS are touched (small results stay;
    cheap tasks are effectively untouched).
  - The task (system + user messages) and the agent's own reasoning/draft
    output are NEVER evicted — only raw tool results.
  - Eviction adds no model turns; it only shrinks the prompt. It cannot run the
    turn count away.
  - Wrapped in try/except: any failure degrades to plain bare behavior, never
    crashes the run.

Tunable via env (defaults are conservative):
  HARNESS_EVICT=0            disable eviction entirely (bare behavior)
  HARNESS_EVICT_WINDOW=<n>   keep the last n turns of tool results in full

steps.result:
# Tool spec — `proxy_eval`

> The mutator invokes this from its shell to triage a deliverable it produced with `run_harvey_agent`.

## Purpose

Grade **ONE deliverable** with a **cheap proxy grader** — a **single mid-tier judge**, NOT the
full cascaded ensemble — to get a fast, **private triage** signal on whether a harness edit
helped. Use it to rank your own attempts mid-rollout; that is its only job.

## Interface

- **Input:** the **deliverable** (a path under `scratch/`, or the text), plus the **rubric /
  criteria** for that task (the same criteria shape found in each `rollouts/<task>/task.md`).
- **Output (under `scratch/`):** a **score** (e.g. pass-rate + per-criterion verdicts) written
  to `scratch/`, e.g. alongside the run dir. Returns the score so you can compare attempts.

## Cost

**Cheaper than the standard ensemble** — one mid-tier judge call per deliverable, no cascade,
no strong-model escalation. **Metered** — see `budget.md`; the unit cost is surfaced by `budget`.

## The critical wall — triage only, NEVER the verdict

This is the load-bearing guard ("no grading your own homework"):

- **Private triage only.** The proxy never feeds selection. A proxy calibrated on one harness
  mis-ranks the moment the harness changes — and could be gamed — so it can only *hint*.
- **Must NEVER read or run the canonical standard eval**, and must never reproduce it. The
  standard eval is run by the **outer driver**, not by any tool you can call.
- **Writes only under `scratch/`. Never writes to `rollouts/`.** No proxy artifact may reach the
  canonical path.
- **Train deliverables only.** You only ever triage attempts you produced on your TRAIN tasks
  (via `run_harvey_agent`). The held-out val set is invisible to you, so it can never be proxy-graded.
- **NO strong-model APIs** (no GPT-5 / Claude / Opus). A single mid-tier judge, full stop. If a
  proxy config blows the budget, write a `.claude/memory/` lesson so the run gets cheaper.
=== check_runaway.md ===
# Tool — `check_runaway`

> The mutator invokes this after a structural edit to catch a turn-count blowup BEFORE committing.

## Purpose

A **hard gate** on the single failure that wastes a whole iteration: a `agent_loop.py` / `tools.py`
edit that makes the agent **run away** (take many more turns than the bare harness) — usually from an
unbounded or stacked enforced step. The eval's **canary aborts the entire iteration** when an edit's
turns exceed **3× the bare baseline**, so check that *yourself* first and reject a runaway edit before
you ship it.

## Interface

- **Input:** the task ids you just tested with `run_harvey_agent` — `check_runaway <task_id> [task_id2 ...]`.
- **What it reads (no cost, local files only):**
  - your edit's turns — `scratch/runs/<task>/turns.txt` (written by `run_harvey_agent`),
  - the bare baseline turns — `rollouts/<task>/iter0/metrics.json` (`turn_count`).
- **Output:** per task, `<edit> turns vs bare <n> = <r>x -> ok | RUNAWAY`. **Exits non-zero (FAIL)
  if ANY task exceeds 3× bare** — that exit code is your automatic "this edit is rejected" signal.

## How to use it (hard rule — see CLAUDE.md §2/§8)

After **any** `agent_loop.py` / `tools.py` edit:
1. `run_harvey_agent <a few tasks>` (a batch) to produce deliverables **and** turn counts.
2. `check_runaway <those same tasks>`.
3. **If it FAILS, the edit is REJECTED** — revert it, give the enforced step a hard cap (it fires a
   fixed small number of times, then the agent finishes), or pick a different lever; then re-test.
   **Do not commit a runaway** — the canary will abort the iteration and you'll have spent the budget
   for nothing.

A prose-only `system_prompt.md` edit can't blow up the loop, so it doesn't need this — but a prose
edit that *pushes the agent to do much more work* can still inflate turns, so checking is cheap insurance.

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""Thin client: proxy-grade ONE deliverable with the cheap single judge, via the host broker.

Usage:  .claude/tools/proxy_eval <task_id> [deliverable_path]
        (deliverable_path defaults to scratch/runs/<task_id>/deliverable.md)

The broker grades host-side with a single mid-tier judge (gpt-5-mini) and returns a pass-rate +
per-criterion verdicts, written to scratch/runs/<task_id>/proxy_score.json. Metered (~$0.02).

TRIAGE ONLY — this is a noisy private hint to help you rank your own attempts, NEVER the verdict.
The canonical standard eval runs in the driver on the held-out val set and is not reachable here.
"""
import json
import os
import sys
import urllib.error
import urllib.request

BROKER = os.environ.get("BROKER_URL", "http://172.17.0.1:4100")
WS = os.environ.get("MUTATOR_WORKSPACE", "/workspace")


def post(path, obj, timeout=600):
    req = urllib.request.Request(
        BROKER + path,

def main():
    if len(sys.argv) < 2:
        print("usage: run_harvey_agent <task_id> [task_id2 ...]   (a BATCH runs concurrently)", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(2)
    task_ids = sys.argv[1:]
    runs = os.path.join(WS, "scratch", "runs")
    os.makedirs(runs, exist_ok=True)
    done_path = os.path.join(runs, ".batch_%s.done" % _batch_id(task_ids))

    if os.environ.get("_RHA_WORKER") == "1":
        try:
            _worker(task_ids, done_path)  # we ARE the detached worker
        except Exception as e:  # a crash must STILL leave a marker, else the mutator polls forever
            _write_done(done_path, "ERROR: run_harvey_agent worker crashed: %s" % e)
        return

    # Foreground: detach a worker that survives the 10-min shell cap, then return immediately.
    try:
        os.remove(done_path)  # clear any stale marker from a prior launch of this same batch
    except OSError:
        pass
    log_path = os.path.join(runs, ".batch_%s.log" % _batch_id(task_ids))
    env = dict(os.environ)
    env["_RHA_WORKER"] = "1"
    try:
        with open(log_path, "w") as lf:
            subprocess.Popen(
                [sys.executable, os.path.abspath(__file__)] + task_ids,
                env=env, stdout=lf, stderr=lf, stdin=subprocess.DEVNULL,
                start_new_session=True,  # new session -> survives the foreground shell being killed at 10 min
            )
    except OSError as e:
        print("could not launch background worker: %s" % e, file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(5)
    print("LAUNCHED %d task(s) in the BACKGROUND (survives the 10-min shell cap)." % len(task_ids))
    print("Deliverables are NOT ready yet — WAIT ~15-20 min for the batch to finish, THEN proxy_eval.")
    print("  poll for done : test -f %s   (marker holds the summary; appears when the batch completes)" % done_path)
    print("  live log      : %s" % log_path)
    print("  deliverables  : scratch/runs/<task>/deliverable.md (+ turns.txt) once done")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

steps.text: I can't inspect the new transcript, but the markup +22% with +8 turns is almost certainly **re-read churn**: on verbatim-heavy redline work the agent needs the source doc throughout, so it re-reads each evicted doc repeatedly. The fix is elegant and general: **make a document "sticky" once it's re-read after eviction** — evict each doc at most once; if the agent proves it still needs it, keep it resident thereafter. This kills the backfire and churn while preserving the big wins on read-once-then-draft tasks. Let me implement it.

call.name: Edit
call.old:
Design points that keep this safe and bounded:
  - Only outputs older than RECENT_WINDOW turns are eligible (the agent has had
    several turns with the doc resident to extract what it needs).
  - Only outputs larger than MIN_EVICT_CHARS are touched (small results stay;
    cheap tasks are effectively untouched).
  - The task (system + user messages) and the agent's own reasoning/draft
    output are NEVER evicted — only raw tool results.
  - Eviction adds no model turns; it only shrinks the prompt. It cannot run the
    turn count away.
  - Wrapped in try/except: any failure degrades to plain bare behavior, never
    crashes the run.
call.new:

    cheap tasks are effectively untouched).
  - **Sticky re-reads.** A document is evicted at most *once*. If the agent
    re-reads a file it had previously evicted, that file is marked sticky and is
    never evicted again — the agent has demonstrated it needs the doc resident
    throughout (e.g. clause-by-clause redline/markup work), so we stop churning

steps.text:
Dev results decode to a critical decision. Let me lay it out:

- **Cost: 4.48M → 3.56M = −20.5%** ✓ clear win
- **Accuracy: 0.8079 → 0.7923 = −1.57 pts** — borderline negative, with a *causal pattern*: the worst drops cluster on short "identify-issues / compare" tasks (employment −13.5, healthcare −10.2, insurance −7.7, compare-fund −7.0) where the whole doc is needed throughout and eviction mid-run loses it.
- **A per-task turn blowup: isda-master-pack 18→60 turns = 3.33×** — this *exceeds the 3× canary threshold*. On val, a similar task would **abort the iteration**. This alone disqualifies the current config.

So I must **not** commit window=5 as-is. Two fixable root causes: (1) eviction hurts small/short tasks for little cost gain — the dollars live in a handful of huge tasks; (2) churn (re-read loops) blows up turns, likely from the same doc read under different path spellings defeating stickiness. My refinement: **a size-gate** (never evict until resident raw context is genuinely large → fully protects small tasks), **basename-keyed stickiness** (kills cross-spelling churn), and a **larger window**.

steps.text: All refinements validated. But I caught a **calibration error**: the size-gate is on *resident raw chars* (hundreds of k), not cumulative input_tokens (millions). At 500k chars it would exempt even the motion task (~400k chars resident) and kill the win. The real variable separating eviction *winners* (long tasks) from *losers* (short tasks where the doc is needed at write-up) is **run length** — which the **window** already controls. Let me make the window the primary short-task protector and keep the size-gate only as a low floor for tiny tasks.

lessons.name: README.md
lessons.body:
# .claude/memory/ — the mutator's verified lessons

**Verified lessons promoted out of `scratch/`** — a finding earns a place here only **after it
survives a held-out check** (it held on a task the edit wasn't tuned on; a budget-kill that
recurred; a proxy config that blew the cap). Working notes and unconfirmed hunches stay in
`scratch/`; this directory is the durable, trusted record carried across iterations.

Read these **first** each run — they are verified findings on this **same** harness, so build on
them rather than re-deriving. But re-check each still holds against the `rollouts/` in front of
you before relying on it (modes shift as the harness changes).

Writable and yours to curate; tracked as mutator state, **not** part of the scored artifact.
